Think of the integers laid out on a line.  
For each element s of a finite set S we must choose either s−1 or s+1 as its partner, and different elements of S must receive different partners that are not themselves in S.  
Do the counting component–by–component, where a component is a connected part of the line containing some elements of S together with all integers at distance 1 from them.  
The number of admissible partnerings for all of S equals the product of the numbers of partnerings for its components.

1.  A single isolated element of S
   ( … □ • □ … )  
   has two choices (left or right), so contributes a factor 2.

2.  Two elements of S separated by exactly one integer  
   ( … • □ • … )  
   share the middle integer; with easy case–work one finds 3 possible
   partnerings.  Generally, an alternating chain of k elements of S  

   • □ • □ • □ ⋯ □ •  

   contributes k+1 partnerings.  (Check k=1→2, k=2→3, k=3→4, etc.)

   No run of three consecutive elements of S is allowed, because the
   middle one would then have no available partner.

The set S is required to have exactly  

4040 = 2³ · 5 · 101  

different cousin sets.

•  To obtain the prime factor 101 we need a component whose
   contribution is exactly 101.  By item 2 this means an alternating
   chain of k elements of S with k+1 = 101, hence k = 100.
   Therefore S must contain at least 100 elements just to supply the
   factor 101.

•  The factor 5 is achieved most economically by an alternating chain
   of 4 elements (contribution 5).  No configuration with 3 or fewer
   elements can contribute 5 (the possibilities for 3 elements are
   2, 4, 6, 8).

•  The factor 2³ is obtained most economically with three isolated
   elements (each contributes 2).

Thus the least possible total size of S is

100 (for the 101-component)  
+ 4 (for the 5-component)  
+ 3 (for the three singletons giving 2³)  
= 107.

An explicit example:

S = {0, 2, 4, …, 198}                 (100 elements, gives factor 101)  
    ∪ {300, 302, 304, 306}            (4 elements, gives factor 5)  
    ∪ {400, 410, 420}                 (3 singletons, give factor 8)

The three groups are separated by at least two vacant integers, so they
form independent components, and the total number of cousin sets is

101 · 5 · 8 = 4040.

Therefore the smallest possible size of S is

\boxed{107}.

Step 1.  What kinds of subsets S work?
If S contains three consecutive integers a , a+1 , a+2 , then the middle one has
neither a-1 nor a+3 available (both differ from it by 1 but belong to S),
so it could not be matched.  
Hence S can never contain three consecutive integers.  
Thus every component of S along the number line is either

• a single isolated integer, or  
• a block of exactly two consecutive integers.

Step 2.  How many choices of T does one component give?

a)  A two–element block {x , x+1}.  
   The only possible matching is x↔x-1 , x+1↔x+2, so this block contributes
exactly 1 choice.

b)  A chain of k isolated integers that are two apart, e.g.  
   {a , a+2 , a+4 , … , a+2(k–1)}.  
   Let every element choose L (= its left neighbour) or R (= its right
   neighbour).  
   A clash occurs only when some element chooses R and the next one chooses L,
   because then they both pick the same integer.  
   Thus the forbidden pattern in the LR–word is “…RL…”, i.e. the binary word
   may not contain “10”.

   Words of length k over {0,1} with no “10” are precisely
         0…0  1…1   (some 0’s followed by some 1’s),
   and there are k+1 of them (choose where the 1’s start, or choose “no 1’s”).
   Therefore such a chain contributes k+1 possible matchings.

Consequently
number of cousins of S   =   ∏ (k_i + 1),
the product running over all chains of isolated integers of lengths k_i .
(Two–element blocks add the factor 1 and can be ignored when we want to
maximise the product.)

Step 3.  Turn the problem into number theory.

Let a_i = k_i + 1  (so every a_i ≥ 2).  
We need
          ∏ a_i  =  4040
and we want to minimise
          |S| = Σ k_i = Σ(a_i – 1) = Σ a_i  –  r
where r is the number of factors.

With a fixed product, splitting a factor a·b into the two factors a and b
changes the sum by

        (a – 1) + (b – 1)  –  (ab – 1)  =  –(a–1)(b–1)   ≤  0,

so the sum (hence |S|) never gets larger, and usually gets smaller, when we
split.  Therefore we should split as much as possible, i.e. factor 4040 into
as many small integers ≥2 as we can.

4040 = 2³ · 5 · 101.  
The primes themselves are already the smallest allowed factors, so the best we
can do is

        a-list :   2 , 2 , 2 , 5 , 101.

Step 4.  Compute the minimum size.

Corresponding k_i ’s are

        1 , 1 , 1 , 4 , 100,

whose sum is

        |S| = 1 + 1 + 1 + 4 + 100 = 107.

Step 5.  Exhibit a set S with 107 elements and 4040 cousins.

For instance, take five separated components:

• {0}  
• {10}  
• {20}  
• {100,102,104,106}          (a chain of length 4)  
• {1000,1002, … , 1188}      (100 integers two apart).

They are far enough apart so that different components never interact, and
their lengths are exactly 1,1,1,4 and 100, giving
      (1+1)(1+1)(1+1)(4+1)(100+1) = 2·2·2·5·101 = 4040
possible partner-sets T.

Therefore 107 cousins can indeed be attained, and by the argument above no
smaller |S| is possible.

\boxed{107}

Step 1.  How many choices does a single element have?  
If an element s ∈ S is NOT consecutive to another element of S, it can be paired with either
s–1 or s+1, so it would seem to have 2 choices.  
Two such elements that are distance 2 apart, however, can both try to use the same
middle integer; the combination “(left element chooses right, right element chooses left)’’
is forbidden.

Step 2.  Decomposing S.  
a)  S can never contain three consecutive integers – the middle one would have no
outside neighbour.  Hence every maximal run of consecutive integers has length 1
(“a singleton’’) or 2 (“a doubleton’’).  

b)  A doubleton {x,x+1} is completely forced: x must pair with x–1 and x+1 with x+2.
It contributes 0 to the count of choices but adds 2 elements to S.

c)  Delete every doubleton.  What remains is a (possibly empty) sequence of singletons,
each separated from the next by a gap of exactly two.  Call such a sequence a
singleton-chain.  If a chain contains k singletons, the forbidden pattern explained in
Step 1 is that a singleton chooses its right neighbour and the next singleton chooses
its left neighbour.  Enumerating all 2^k raw choices and eliminating the k–1 forbidden
ones gives

        number of legal matchings for a chain of k singletons = k+1.             (★)

Therefore

        number of cousin sets of S  =  ∏ (k_i+1),

where the product runs over all singleton-chains of S and k_i is the length of the i-th
chain.

Step 3.  Relating the size of S and the product.  
Let the factors in (★) be a_1,a_2,…,a_r (each a_i ≥ 2).  
Write k_i=a_i–1; then

        |S| = (k_1+⋯+k_r) + 2·(# of doubletons)  
             = (a_1+⋯+a_r) – r + 2·(# of doubletons).

Adding doubletons can only increase |S| while leaving the product unchanged, so to
minimise |S| we take no doubletons at all.  Hence

        |S| = (a_1+⋯+a_r) – r,  with  a_1a_2⋯a_r = 4040.             (1)

Step 4.  Factoring 4040 economically.  
4040 = 2^3·5·101.

For a fixed product, the quantity (a–1)/a is smallest when a=2, so to minimise the sum
in (1) we want as many 2’s as possible, except that the prime factors 5 and 101 must
each be contained in some a_i.

The best choice is therefore

        a_1=101, a_2=5, a_3=a_4=a_5=2,

whose product is 4040.

Then

        (a_1+⋯+a_5) – 5  =  (101+5+2+2+2) – 5  = 112 – 5 = 107.

Any other factorisation either replaces some 2’s by larger numbers (raising the sum) or
combines several of the present factors into one larger factor (raising the sum even
more).  Hence 107 is the absolute minimum possible value of |S|.

Step 5.  Constructing an example of size 107.  
Take

• a chain of 100 singletons: {0,2,4,…,198};  
• a chain of 4 singletons: {300,302,304,306};  
• three isolated singletons: {400,404,408}.

These chains are separated by gaps of at least 3, so there are no interactions.  By (★)

        #cousins = (100+1)(4+1)·2·2·2 = 101·5·8 = 4040.

Thus a set S with 107 elements exists having exactly 4040 cousins, and no smaller
set can do so.

\boxed{107}

Step 1.  Basic observations  
For every integer s ∈ S its candidate partners are the two neighbours s – 1 and s + 1 (if they are not themselves in S).  
Draw a bipartite graph whose left–hand vertices are the elements of S and whose right–hand vertices are all these candidates; join s to those neighbours that are not in S.  
A set T is a cousin of S  ⇔  this graph contains a perfect matching that saturates S; T is then exactly the right-hand vertices used by that matching.

Step 2.  Forbidden patterns in S  
If three consecutive integers were all in S then the middle one would have no available partner – hence blocks of 3 (or more) consecutive elements are impossible.  
Thus every connected component of the graph is a path whose S–part is made of  
• isolated single elements …#…   or  
• double blocks …##… .

Step 3.  How many cousins can one component contribute?  
Take a component that contains m elements of S.

• If it contains a double block then every pairing in that block is forced, so the whole component contributes only 1 possible T–set.

• If it is a chain of m isolated singles, then there are m + 1 candidate vertices (one on each end and one between every two singles).  
To obtain a perfect matching we must choose exactly m of those m + 1 candidates – i.e. decide which single candidate to leave out.  
Hence such a chain contributes exactly m + 1 cousin sets.

Therefore, for every component

   number of cousin sets  ≤  (number of S–elements in the component) + 1.              (★)

Step 4.  A 101–factor is indispensable  
4040 = 8·5·101 contains the prime 101, so some component has to contribute a factor 101.  
By (★) that component must have at least 100 elements of S, and a chain of 100 isolated singles indeed gives exactly 101 cousins.  Consequently

                     |S| ≥ 100.                                                        (1)

Step 5.  What is the smallest number of extra elements needed for the remaining factor 40?  
Remove those 100 elements; the remaining elements of S must yield altogether 40 cousins.  
Write their sizes as m1, m2,…; then by (★)

     (m1+1)(m2+1)… = 40 and m1+m2+… = r  (the number of remaining elements).

Because 2^5 = 32 < 40 ≤ 2^6, at least 6 elements are required.  
A complete check of all partitions of 6 elements shows that none can give the product 40.  
With 7 elements the partition 4 + 1 + 1 + 1 yields  
(4+1)(1+1)(1+1)(1+1) = 5·2·2·2 = 40,  
and no partition of 7 elements gives a larger product.  Hence

                     r_min = 7.                                                       (2)

Step 6.  Lower bound for |S|  
From (1) and (2):

                     |S| ≥ 100 + 7 = 107.                                             (3)

Step 7.  A set S with 107 elements and exactly 4040 cousins  
Construct S as the disjoint union of the following components.

1.  A chain of 100 isolated singles  
  {0, 2, 4, …, 198}.  
 This contributes 101 cousin sets.

2.  A chain of 4 isolated singles  
  {300, 302, 304, 306}.  
 This contributes 5 cousin sets.

3.  Three further isolated singles  
  {400}, {404}, {408}.  
 Each contributes 2 cousin sets.

(The large gaps ensure the components are independent.)

Total number of cousins  
 101 × 5 × 2 × 2 × 2 = 4040.

Step 8.  Minimality  
Inequality (3) shows that no set with fewer than 107 elements can have 4040 cousins, while the construction above achieves 4040 cousins with 107 elements.  Therefore 107 is minimal.

\boxed{107}

Step 1.  What a “cousin’’ looks like.  
For every \(s\in S\) its partner in a cousin set \(T\) must be \(s-1\) or \(s+1\), and no two different elements of \(S\) may choose the same partner.  Thus for every choice of such partners we obtain exactly one cousin \(T\).

Step 2.  How the shape of \(S\) limits the choices.

•  If three consecutive integers were in \(S\) then the middle one would have no available partner, so every block of consecutive integers in \(S\) can have length at most \(2\).

•  A block ‘SS’ (two consecutive integers) forces its partners (\(s-1\) for the left one, \(s+2\) for the right one).  Hence such a block contributes a factor \(1\) to the number of cousins.

•  An isolated element ‘S’ has two possible partners and contributes a factor \(2\).

•  If we have a chain of \(n\ge 1\) isolated elements separated by single gaps  
\(S\;\_\;S\;\_\;S\;\_\;\dots \_\;S\)   
(the component then contains \(n\) elements of \(S\) and \(n+1\) outside vertices),
the number of admissible partner-choices for this component is

\[
f(n)=n+1 .
\]

Indeed, letting the first \(S\) choose its left outside neighbour leaves a chain of \(n-1\) isolated \(S\)’s, giving \(f(n-1)\) possibilities; letting it choose its right neighbour triggers a “domino’’ of forced choices and contributes \(1\).  Hence \(f(n)=f(n-1)+1\) with \(f(1)=2\), so \(f(n)=n+1\).

Thus every component of \(S\) contributes a factor

 • \(1\)  (from a ‘SS’ block), or  
 • \(n+1\) with \(n\ge 1\)  (from a chain of \(n\) isolated elements).

Step 3.  What factors are needed.

The wanted number of cousins is

\[
4040 = 2^{3}\cdot 5 \cdot 101 .
\]

Since \(101\) is prime, one of the components must contribute a factor \(101\); therefore we need a chain with \(n+1=101\), i.e. \(n=100\) isolated elements.  So \(S\) already contains at least \(100\) isolated elements.

We still have to supply a factor \(5\cdot 2^{3}=40\).

•  The smallest way to obtain a factor \(5\) is a chain of \(n=4\) isolated elements (factor \(n+1=5\)); this uses \(4\) elements of \(S\).

•  The smallest way to obtain a factor \(2^{3}=8\) is with three isolated elements in three separate components, each giving a factor \(2\); this uses \(3\) more elements of \(S\).

Adding anything else (more isolated elements or any ‘SS’ blocks) would only increase the size of \(S\) without reducing the required factors.

Step 4.  Constructing an optimal set.

Place the components far apart so they do not interact:

1.  Positions \(0,2,4,\dots ,198\)  (100 isolated elements) ⟶ factor \(101\).
2.  Positions \(1000,1002,1004,1006\)  (4 isolated elements in a single chain) ⟶ factor \(5\).
3.  Positions \(2000,\;3000,\;4000\)  (3 isolated singletons) ⟶ factor \(2^{3}=8\).

Altogether
\[
\#S = 100+4+3 = 107,
\qquad
\text{number of cousins } = 101\cdot 5\cdot 8 = 4040.
\]

Step 5.  Minimality.

•  A factor \(101\) forces at least \(100\) isolated elements.  
•  A factor \(5\) cannot be produced with fewer than \(4\) isolated elements.  
•  A factor \(8\) cannot be produced with fewer than \(3\) isolated elements.  
•  ‘SS’ blocks never increase the number of cousins, so they only enlarge \(|S|\).

Consequently every set \(S\) with exactly \(4040\) cousins must contain at least  
\(100+4+3 = 107\) elements, and the construction above shows that this bound is attained.

\[
\boxed{107}
\]

Let S contain k integers.  
If every element of S is at distance at least 3 from the others then (for every s∈S) the two possible partners s−1 and s+1 are all different and are not in S, so each element may be matched independently to its left or to its right; consequently the number of cousins of S equals 2^k.  

Hence the number of cousins of any k-element set never exceeds 2^k, so

 2^k ≥ 4040 > 2^11 = 2048   ⇒ k ≥ 12. …(1)

It remains to show that k = 12 can actually be attained.

————————————————————————
A 12–element set that has exactly 4040 cousins
————————————————————————
Take

 B = {0,2,4,6}  (size 4, the members are 2 units apart)  
 C = {9,11,13,15,17,19,21,23,25} (size 9, again spacing 2)

and let  

 S = B ∪ C \{9}                (so |S| = 12).

(The element 9 is removed so that B and C are disjoint; the distances in C
are still exactly 2.)

1.  The 3 elements of B are at pairwise distance ≥3 from every other element
    of S, so each of them contributes a factor 2 to the number of cousins.
    Thus B contributes 2^3 = 8.

2.  The 9 elements of C form a path in which successive elements are
    2 units apart.  
    For such a path of length n the valid left/right choices are exactly the
    (n + 1) strings of the form L…LR…R (no R immediately followed by L).  
    Hence the 9–term path contributes 9 + 1 = 10.

Consequently

 (number of cousins of S) = 8 · 505 = 4040.

————————————————————————
Conclusion
————————————————————————
Inequality (1) shows that |S| ≥ 12, and the set S constructed above
shows that 12 is attainable.  Therefore the least possible number of
elements of a set with exactly 4040 cousins is

\boxed{12}.

Let S be a finite set of integers and write its elements in increasing order  
a₁ < a₂ < … < a_n.

For every s∈S we must choose one of the two neighbours s–1 or s+1 to put into the cousin set T, and all chosen neighbours must be distinct and outside S.  
Record the choice for each element as

L  (if s–1 is taken)  or  
R  (if s+1 is taken).

When two elements of S differ by at least 3, their choices can never collide; decisions for such elements are independent.

The only possible collisions happen inside “clusters’’ of elements that are at distance 2 from one another:

x , x+2 , x+4 , … , x+2(k–1) (k ≥ 1).

(Three consecutive integers cannot all lie in S, otherwise the middle one would have no available neighbour.)

Inside such a chain of k elements the only forbidden pattern is “RL’’:
choosing R for an element forces the next element (two units to the right) not to choose L, because both would then want the same integer x+2i–1.

Thus a legal sequence of choices inside the chain is a string of L’s followed by
a string of R’s; there can be at most one switch from L to R and never a switch
back.  There are therefore exactly k+1 admissible choice-strings.

Hence

Cluster of k elements spaced 2 apart  →  contributes factor (k+1)
to the number of cousins.

If two elements of S are consecutive, their orientations are forced
(left element must choose its left neighbour, right element its right neighbour),
so such a 2-block would contribute only a factor 1 and never helps in reaching a
large prescribed product.  We therefore omit consecutive elements when we wish
to minimise |S|.

------------------------------------------------
Counting cousins

Let the clusters of S (separated from each other by gaps of at least 3, so they
do not interfere) have sizes k₁, k₂, …, k_m.  
Then

number of cousin sets  =  ∏_{i=1}^{m} (k_i + 1).

We are told this product equals 4040, and we want the smallest possible total
size

|S| = k₁ + k₂ + … + k_m = Σ (k_i + 1) – m.

So we must write 4040 as a product of integers ≥ 2 and make the **sum**
of those integers as small as possible; subtracting the (fixed) number of
factors m will then give the minimal |S|.

Breaking a factor ab (a,b ≥ 2) into two separate factors a and b lowers the
sum from (ab) to (a+b) (< ab).  Hence the minimal sum is achieved when every
factor is prime.

Prime factorisation:
4040 = 2³ · 5 · 101 = 2 · 2 · 2 · 5 · 101.

Therefore take

k-values k = 1, 1, 1, 4, 100

(one chain of 100 elements, one of 4 elements, and three isolated elements,
with gaps of at least three between all chains).  
They give factors

k+1 = 2, 2, 2, 5, 101,

whose product is the required 4040.

Total number of elements in S:

|S| = 1 + 1 + 1 + 4 + 100 = 107.

No product representation of 4040 can yield a smaller total, so 107 is minimal.

------------------------------------------------
Final answer
\boxed{107}

Step 1 –  Cutting the line into independent blocks  
Put the integers on a line.  
Two elements of S that are at distance 3 or more from each other have completely disjoint
possible partners (they use neighbours ±1 which are at least two units apart).
Hence the number C(S) of cousin–sets of S is the product of the numbers contributed by every
maximal block of S in which successive elements are only 1 or 2 apart.

So, write S as a disjoint union of such blocks  
B₁ ,B₂ , … ,B_r ; then C(S)=∏ C(B_i).

Step 2 –  How many cousins can one block have?  
Consider one block B whose elements (red) lie in order
…–B₀–R₁–B₁–R₂–B₂–…–R_k–B_k –…  
where the B’s are the (blue) integers lying between the elements of B.
Let  

a_j = # matchings of the first j red vertices in which B_j is free,  
b_j = # matchings of the first j red vertices in which B_j is already used.

With the obvious initial values a₁=b₁=1 the recurrence
a_{j+1}=a_j and b_{j+1}=a_j+b_j
holds, giving a_j=1 and b_j=j.  Hence for a block that contains k elements of S  

C(B)=a_k+b_k = 1+k.                                               (1)

Equality (1) is attained exactly when successive elements of the block are
two units apart (no two are consecutive); if B contains a consecutive pair,
some red vertex is forced and the number of matchings is strictly smaller.
Therefore every block with t elements contributes at most t+1 cousins.

Step 3 –  Where does the factor 101 come from?  
4040 = 2³ · 5 · 101 contains the prime 101, so some block must contribute a
factor 101.  By (1) that block must have at least 100 elements, and a block
of exactly 100 elements arranged 0,2,4,…,198 contributes 100+1 = 101 cousins.
Thus  

|S| ≥ 100.                                                         (2)

Step 4 –  Producing the remaining factor 40 as economically as possible  
After removing the factor 101 there remain 40 = 2³·5 to be supplied by the
other blocks.

• A single element of S (isolated by at least three units from the rest)
is a block of size 1 and, by (1), contributes 2 cousins.  
• No block with 1, 2 or 3 elements can contribute a factor 5
(checking the possibilities 1,2,3,4,6,8).  
• The smallest block that supplies a 5 is one of four elements spaced
2 units apart; by (1) it gives 4+1 =5 cousins.

Hence the least number of elements that can realise the needed factor 40 is

 four elements for the factor 5,  
 three isolated elements for the factor 2³,

altogether 4+3 = 7 elements.                                    (3)

Step 5 –  Lower bound for |S|  
Combining (2) and (3) we obtain

 |S| ≥ 100 + 7 = 107.                                            (4)

Step 6 –  A set S with exactly 107 elements and 4040 cousins  
Take

• A block A = {0,2,4,…,198} (100 elements, spaced two apart) → 101 cousins;  
• A block B = {1000,1002,1004,1006} (4 elements, spaced two apart) → 5 cousins;  
• Three isolated elements C = {2000, 2003, 2006} → 2·2·2 = 8 cousins.

The gaps between the blocks are ≥3, so the numbers multiply:

C(S)=101·5·8 = 4040.

Thus a set S with 107 elements having exactly 4040 cousins exists.

Step 7 –  Conclusion  
Because 107 elements are necessary and sufficient, the least possible size of S is

\boxed{107}.

Let S be a finite set of integers and let  
c(S) = the number of sets T that satisfy the three “cousin” conditions with S.

1.  How many cousins can one element contribute?  
 For an isolated element s (that is, both s–1 and s+1 are not in S) the map s↦s–1 or s↦s+1 is free, giving 2 possibilities.

2.  What happens if two elements of S are neighbours?  
 If a,b∈S with b=a+1, then a can only be matched with a–1 and b only with b+1, so that pair contributes exactly 1 possibility.

3.  The useful connected pattern  
 Suppose a component of S consists of n elements forming the arithmetic progression  
 s, s+2, s+4, … , s+2(n–1)  
 (all gaps are 2).  
 Label the elements x1,x2,…,xn from left to right.  
 Each xi may be matched to its left neighbour Li or its right neighbour Ri.
 The only forbidden situation is “R followed by L’’ (two successive xi choosing the same middle number).  
 Counting length-n strings over {L,R} with no RL gives the recurrence  
 L1=R1=1, Li=Li-1, Ri=Li-1+Ri-1,  
 so total strings Ti=Li+Ri satisfy T1=2,T2=3 and Ti=Ti-1+1.  Hence  

 Ti = i + 1.             (∗)

 Therefore such a component of n elements always contributes exactly n+1 cousins, and no connected component with the same n can contribute more (any gap 1 introduces forced choices and only lowers the count).

4.  Decomposing S into components  
 If S is split by gaps of at least 3, the different components are independent, so  
 c(S)=∏(c(component)).

5.  Reaching 4040 cousins with as few elements as possible  
 Factor 4040 = 2³·5·101 (101 prime).

 •  A factor 101 must come from a single component (because 101 is prime).  
  By (∗) this needs n+1 = 101  ⇒  n = 100 elements.

 •  A factor 5 is achieved most economically by a component of 4 elements arranged 2 apart  
  (giving 4+1 = 5 cousins).

 •  A factor 2 is achieved most economically by an isolated element.  
  Three isolated elements contribute 2·2·2 = 8.

 These give 101·5·8 = 4040 cousins with  
 100 + 4 + 3 = 107 elements.

6.  Showing 107 is minimal  
 – The component giving the factor 101 needs at least 100 elements, as no connected
   component of fewer than 100 can yield more than n+1 ≤ 100 cousins.  
 – To supply the remaining factor 40, the information‐theoretic bound 2^k ≥ 40 shows at least  
   k ≥ 6 elements are needed, and because 5 is prime, one needs at least 4 of those
   elements in a 5-cousin component and at least 3 more elements to contribute the factor 8.  
   Hence ≥100+7 = 107 elements are required.

7.  Explicit minimal set  

 Component A (100 elements, gap 2) : {0,2,4,…,198}  
 Component B (4 elements, gap 2)   : {300,302,304,306}  
 Components C₁,C₂,C₃ (singles)    : {500}, {1000}, {1500}

 These 107 integers form a set S with exactly  
 (100+1)·(4+1)·2·2·2 = 101·5·8 = 4040 cousins.

Hence the least possible size of S is  

\boxed{107}

Let a finite set S of integers be fixed.  
Look at the bipartite graph whose left–hand vertices are the elements of S and whose right–hand vertices are the integers not in S.  An edge joins s ∈ S to an integer t ∉ S when |s–t|=1.  A “cousin’’ T of S is exactly a choice of a perfect matching in this graph that covers every vertex of S; the chosen right–hand vertices form T.

1.  Structure of a connected component  
   Along the number line, two consecutive outside–of–S integers break the graph, so each connected component is an interval of the form  
        O  S  O  S  …  O  S  O  
   where O denotes “outside of S’’ and S denotes “in S’’, with no two consecutive O’s inside the component and no run of three consecutive S’s (otherwise a middle S would have no outside neighbour).

   The only components that allow any matching are:

   a) an alternating block with n≥1 S–vertices separated by single O–vertices  
        O  (S O)^{n–1}  S  O   
      (length = 2n+1)

   b) a pair SS bracketed by outside vertices  
        O  S  S  O

   No longer run of consecutive S’s is matchable.

2.  Number of matchings in an alternating block  
   Number the S–vertices 1,…,n from left to right, write 0 if an S is matched to its left neighbour and 1 if matched to its right neighbour.  
   The interior outside vertex between S_i and S_{i+1} can be used by at most one of them, so the forbidden pattern is 10.  Hence the binary strings permissible are the non-decreasing strings 0^a1^{n–a}.  There are n+1 of them.

   Therefore

        (alternating block with n S’s)  ⇒  n+1 matchings.

   In the SS block every S is forced to its unique outside neighbour, so it contributes a factor 1 and will never help to increase the count.  Consequently an optimal S will be a disjoint union of alternating blocks only.

3.  Factorisation problem  
   If S is the disjoint union of alternating blocks with sizes n_1, …, n_k, then

        number of cousins  =  Π (n_i + 1),
        |S|                =  Σ n_i.

   Here we need the product to be 4040 = 2^3·5·101.

   Because 101 is prime, one of the factors n_i+1 must be 101, so one block needs n=100 elements.

   Remove this factor.  The remaining product to realise is 40 = 2^3·5 coming from factors ≥2.  
   Writing 40 as a product of integers ≥2 while minimising Σ(n_i) = Σ(factor–1) is equivalent to partitioning the multiset {2,2,2,5}.  The minimum sum of the factors themselves is obtained by keeping every prime separate:

        factors : 2, 2, 2, 5
        sizes   : 1, 1, 1, 4   (because n_i = factor – 1)

   giving a total of 1+1+1+4 = 7 additional elements.  Any grouping of primes into larger factors increases the total size.

   Hence the minimum possible total size is

        |S| = 100 + 7 = 107.

4.  Construction attaining 107  
   Take five disjoint alternating blocks, place them far apart on the number line so they do not interact:

   • one block of the form  …, 0, 1, 0, 1, …, 0  with 100 S’s (factor 101);  
   • one block 0 1 0 1 0 1 0 1 0 with 4 S’s (factor 5);  
   • three isolated S’s each with the pattern 0 1 0 (factors 2,2,2).

   Their factors multiply to 101·5·2·2·2 = 4040 and they contain 100+4+1+1+1 = 107 integers.

Therefore the smallest possible size of a set S that has exactly 4040 cousins is

\boxed{107}

We interpret a cousin set T of a finite integer set S as follows.

• For every s∈S there is exactly one t∈T with |s−t|=1.  
• |T|=|S| and S∩T=∅.

Place the integers on a line.  
Write an element of S as “●’’ and an integer that is not in S as “○’’.

Consecutive ●’s may not form blocks of length ≥3, because the middle ● would have both neighbors in S and would have no permissible partner in T.  
Hence every connected component of S on the line is either  

1. a singleton ● –●–● (distance ≥2 to the next ●), or  
2. a doubleton ●● (two consecutive ●’s).

Doubletons give no choice: the left ● must pair to the left, the right ● to the right, so a doubleton contributes a factor 1 to the number of cousins.

Choice only occurs inside chains of singletons that are spaced exactly two units apart

 ● ○ ● ○ … ○ ● .

If such a chain contains ℓ singletons, label them B1,…,Bℓ from left to right.  
For every i (1<i≤ℓ) the white vertex between Bi−1 and Bi can be used by at most one of them, and if Bi−1 takes it then Bi is forced to take its right neighbour.  
Writing L (left choice) and R (right choice) for every Bi we get sequences satisfying

 “R must be followed by R; after L we may write L or R.”

All legal sequences are therefore determined by the position k (0≤k≤ℓ) of the first R:

 LL…L︸k RR…R︸ℓ−k.

Hence the number of different cousin sets contributed by such a chain is ℓ+1.

Result.  
If S decomposes into chains of lengths ℓ1,ℓ2,…,ℓm (singletons) and any number of doubletons, then

 #(cousins of S)=∏ (ℓi+1).                        (★)

There is no other source of choice; doubletons contribute the factor 1.

------------------------------------------------
We are told #(cousins of S)=4040=2³·5·101.

To obtain the factor 101 in (★) we need one factor ℓ+1=101, i.e. a chain of length 100; this already contributes 100 elements to S, so |S|≥100.

The remaining factor is 40=2³·5.  
We must write 40 as a product of integers ≥2 and minimise the accompanying sum of the corresponding ℓ’s.

Factorisation   product   sum of ℓ’s  
40         40      39  
20·2       40      19+1=20  
10·4       40      9+3=12  
8·5        40      7+4=11  
5·4·2       40      4+3+1=8  
5·2·2·2      40      4+1+1+1=7  ← least

Thus the smallest additional number of singletons is 7 (three isolated singletons and one chain of length 4).

Minimum size of S:

 100 (from the 101-chain)  
+ 4 (chain length 4 → factor 5)  
+ 3 (three isolated singletons → factor 2³)  
= 107.

------------------------------------------------
Construction attaining 107.

Take

C₁: 0,2,4,…,198  (100 singletons spaced by 2)  
C₂: 300,304,308  (three isolated singletons)  
C₃: 400,402,404,406 (chain of length 4).

The gaps between the components exceed 2, so by (★) the number of cousins is

 (100+1)·(1+1)·(1+1)·(1+1)·(4+1)=101·2³·5=4040.

Therefore the least possible size of S is

\boxed{107}.

Let S be a finite set of integers and let  
c(S) = number of sets T that are cousins of S.

How to count c(S)

1.  No three consecutive integers can all lie in S.  
    (The middle element would have no available neighbour, so c(S)=0.)

2.  Look along the number line and mark the elements of S.  
    •  If two elements of S are consecutive, each of them is forced to use
       the outside neighbour; that consecutive pair contributes a factor 1 to c(S).  
    •  If an element of S is isolated (distance ≥3 from any other element of S) it may
       choose its left or right neighbour freely; that single contributes a factor 2.  
    •  Suppose we meet a “chain’’ of S–elements spaced exactly two apart:

          x , x+2 , x+4 , … , x+2k        (k ≥ 1)

       No other element of S is at distance ≤2 from the chain’s ends.
       Writing down the possible matchings gives the recursion
           f(1)=2 , f(k)=1+f(k−1)   (k≥2)
       so f(k)=k+1.   
       Hence such a chain of length k (that is, k elements of S) contributes
       the factor k+1 to c(S).

Thus

c(S)=2^(# isolated singles) × ∏(k_i+1),

where each k_i+1 comes from one of the “distance-2’’ chains described above
(consecutive pairs give the factor 1 and may be ignored).

Prime-factor requirements

4040 = 2^3 · 5 · 101

To obtain the factor 101 we need one chain whose contribution is 101,
so k+1 = 101 ⟹ k = 100.
That chain contains 100 elements of S.

To obtain the factor 5 we need one chain contributing 5,
so k+1 = 5 ⟹ k = 4.
That chain contains 4 elements of S.

The remaining factor 2^3 is produced most economically by three isolated
single elements (each gives a factor 2, and each costs one element of S).

Therefore the least possible size of S is

100 (for the 101-chain)  
+  4 (for the 5-chain)  
+  3 (for the three singles)  
= 107.

Construction that attains 107

Take, for example,

A = {0,2,4,…,198}                     (100 elements; distance-2 chain)
B = {202,204,206,208}                 (4 elements; distance-2 chain)
C = {400,404,408}                     (3 isolated singles)

S = A ∪ B ∪ C  has 107 elements and

c(S)=101·5·2·2·2 = 4040.

Since we have shown 107 elements are necessary and a set of that size exists,
the minimum possible number of elements in S is

\boxed{107}.

Step 1.  Basic observations  
For every element s of S the partner that matches it in T must be either s−1 or s+1.  
Hence if two elements of S are at distance at least 3 their possible partners are
distinct and the two elements act independently (2 choices each).  
If two elements of S are at distance 2 they compete for the middle integer; a chain
of k such “alternating” elements  

  s , s+2 , s+4 , … , s+2(k−1)                      (1)

has exactly k+1 different partner–sets, a number independent of the position of the
chain.  No connected component of the line containing only elements of S can have
three consecutive integers, because the middle one would have no available partner.

Thus every connected component of the integer line induced by S is of one of the
following two kinds

 A. an isolated single element                  →  2 possible partners  
 B. a chain of the form (1) with k≥2 elements →  k+1 possible partners

If a component contains k elements it produces at most k+1 partner–sets; this
bound is attained exactly by the chains of kind B.

Because different components are independent, the total number C(S) of cousins of
S equals the product of the numbers contributed by its components.

Step 2.  A prime factor forces a long chain  
4040 = 2³ · 5 · 101 contains the prime 101.  
Because C(S) is a product of the (small) integers 2 and k+1 (k≥2), the prime 101
must itself be one of the factors; hence S must contain a component whose number
of partner–sets is 101.  By the bound “≤ k+1” this requires

 k + 1 = 101  ⇒  k = 100,

so S must contain a chain of the form (1) with exactly 100 elements.  
That already uses 100 elements of S.

Step 3.  What is still missing  
Removing the factor 101 from 4040 leaves 4040 / 101 = 40 = 2³ · 5.  
We still have to realise a factor 40 by means of further components, using as few
additional elements as possible.

Step 4.  Minimising the elements needed for the factor 40  
• An isolated single supplies the factor 2 with one element.  
• The least number of elements that can give the factor 5 is 4 
   (the chain k=4 has 5 partner–sets and contains exactly 4 elements).  
Therefore the factor 40 = 2³ · 5 can be obtained with

 – one chain of length 4 (4 elements, factor 5)  
 – three isolated singles       (3 elements, factor 2·2·2)

Thus the extra factor 40 needs at least 4 + 3 = 7 elements.  (With 6 elements the
maximal factor one can reach is 2⁶ = 64, but 40 itself is impossible because
the factor 5 alone already costs 4 elements and would leave at most 2 elements,
i.e. a maximal extra factor 2² = 4, so 5·4 = 20 < 40.)

Consequently no set S with fewer than 100 + 7 = 107 elements can have 4040 cousins.

Step 5.  Constructing a set with 107 elements and exactly 4040 cousins  
Take the disjoint union of the following components, widely separated on the
integer line so that they do not interfere with one another:

1.  A chain of 100 elements  
  {0, 2, 4, … , 198}                → factor 101

2.  A chain of 4 elements  
  {300, 302, 304, 306}              → factor 5

3.  Three isolated singles  
  {1000}, {1003}, {1006}            → factors 2, 2, 2

The numbers of partner–sets multiply:

 101 · 5 · 2 · 2 · 2 = 4040.

So this S has 100 + 4 + 3 = 107 elements and exactly 4040 cousins.

Step 6.  Conclusion  
We have exhibited a set with 107 elements that has 4040 cousins, and we have shown
that no set with fewer than 107 elements can do so.

\boxed{107}

Let us place every integer on an infinite line and mark the elements of  
S on that line.  
For every s ∈ S there are at most two “candidate” positions that can be used
for the required partner in T, namely s − 1 and s + 1.
A set T is a cousin of S iff one can pick, for every s, exactly one of its
candidates so that

• all chosen candidates are different (T is a set),  
• none of the chosen positions is already occupied by S (T∩S=∅).

Thus the problem is purely local along the number line.  
Break the line into connected components obtained after deleting the points of S.
Inside one of those components we see a string of k consecutive vacancies
bordered by two consecutive elements of S,
           ● 0 1 2 … k−1 ●
where the black dots “●” are elements of S and the numbered places are
vacancies.  
Exactly the same picture appears anywhere two consecutive elements of S
are separated by k vacant integers.

Along that string the two bordering elements of S may use some of the k
vacancies as partners; those vacancies that are not used must be partners
for interior points of S coming from adjacent strings, so altogether the
k vacancies will finally be split into two disjoint (possibly empty) blocks,
one block paired to the left dot and the other block paired to the right
dot.  There are k+1 different ways to split the string, hence it contributes
k+1 possibilities to the total number of cousin sets.

Therefore

C(S)=∏(k_i+1),

where k_i runs through the lengths of all vacancy-strings that lie between
consecutive elements of S.

We are told C(S)=4040 and are asked for the smallest possible |S|.

Factor 4040:
4040 = 2³ · 5 · 101.

So 4040 has 5 factors greater than 1:
2,2,2,5,101.
To realise the product ∏(k_i+1)=4040 we have to write every factor as
(k_i+1).  The vacancy lengths will thus be
1,1,1,4,100   (since 1+1=2 etc.).

Hence we need five vacancy-strings and therefore six elements of S
(the strings sit between consecutive elements of S).
No smaller number of strings can yield a product as large as 4040, so
six elements is minimal.

One concrete example is

S = {0, 2, 4, 6, 11, 112}.

The vacancy lengths between consecutive elements are
1,1,1,4,100, giving (1+1)(1+1)(1+1)(4+1)(100+1)=2·2·2·5·101 = 4040
cousin sets.

Hence the least possible size of S is

\boxed{6}.

Think of the integers arranged on a line.  
Fix a finite set S (its elements are the black vertices).  
For every s ∈ S we must choose one of its neighbours s–1 or s+1
(the chosen neighbour will belong to the cousin set T).  
The choice must satisfy two conditions:

1. the chosen neighbours are all different (T has |S| elements);  
2. none of the chosen neighbours belongs to S (S and T are disjoint).

Label an element of S

• single if the nearest other element of S is at distance ≥3;  
• a member of a 2-chain if its two neighbours are …,s–2,s,s+2,… (i.e. the
elements of S in the chain are spaced by 2).

A chain consisting of k elements of S that are spaced 2 apart contributes

 k+1

different ways to choose the neighbours (write the choices L/L/… then R/R/…, so everything is determined by the first R, hence k+1 possibilities).

A single element contributes 2 possibilities (left or right).

Therefore, if

 S = (chains of lengths c1 , c2 , … , cm)  ∪  (r singles),

the number of cousins of S equals

 ( c1+1 )( c2+1 )⋯( cm+1 ) · 2^r.             (★)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Producing the factor 101  
--------------------------------------------------------------------
101 is prime and odd.  
Because every single contributes the even factor 2, no singles can appear
in whatever part of S is responsible for the factor 101.
Thus that part of S must be one chain, say of length c, with

 c + 1 = 101  ⇒  c = 100.

So at least 100 elements of S are needed just for the factor 101,
and a single 100-element 2-chain indeed supplies that factor.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Producing the remaining factor 40 = 5·2^3  
--------------------------------------------------------------------
From (★) an odd factor 5 must come from a chain, and the only possibility is

 chain length 4 (because 4 + 1 = 5).

That costs 4 more elements.

The remaining factor 2^3=8 must come from singles, and each single supplies
one factor 2, so three singles are required.  
That costs another 3 elements.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Minimal size of S  
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Hence the fewest elements altogether are

 100 (for the 101-chain) + 4 (for the 5-chain) + 3 (singles) = 107.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Explicit construction with 4040 cousins  
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Take

• the 101-factor chain       S₁ = { 0,2,4,…,198 };             |S₁| = 100  
• the  5-factor chain        S₂ = { 300,302,304,306 };         |S₂| = 4  
• the three singles          S₃ = { 500,506,512 }.             |S₃| = 3  

Let S = S₁ ∪ S₂ ∪ S₃.  
The components are far apart, so their choices are independent.  
By (★)

 (# cousins of S) = (100 + 1) · (4 + 1) · 2³ = 101·5·8 = 4040.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Therefore the least possible number of elements of a set S having exactly
4040 cousins is

\boxed{107}.

We first analyse how the choice of the neighbour ( −1  or +1 ) for every element of S determines the cousin set T.

1.  Split the integers into connected components with respect to S.  
    Two elements of S lie in the same component exactly when the distance
    between them is 1 or 2.  
    (If the distance is 3 or larger, the two elements of S are separated
    by at least one integer that is not adjacent to any element of S, so they belong to different components.)

2.  The number of possible choices for the elements of T coming from different
    components multiply, so it suffices to know, for every component,
    how many ways its elements can be matched with their neighbours.

3.  A component can have only the following shapes.

    a)  Adjacent pair “SS”.
        The two elements are forced to match outward,
        hence it contributes 1 to the total count.

    b)  Single element with both neighbours free “ 0 S 0 ”.
        It contributes 2 possibilities.

    c)  Chain  
        S 0 S 0 … 0 S  
        (the distance between consecutive elements of the chain is 2).
        If the chain contains m elements of S, it contributes exactly m+1
        possibilities.
        (The leftmost S may go to its left neighbour or to the middle 0;
         once that choice is fixed the rest of the chain is forced,
         giving m+1 choices altogether.)

    No other pattern can occur, because three consecutive S’s would make
    the middle one unusable.

    Consequently, every component contributes a factor taken from the set  
       {1, 2, 3, 4, …} ,
    where the only way to obtain the value k ( k≥3 ) is from a chain of
    length k−1.

4.  Factorising 4040 we obtain  
       4040 = 2³ · 5 · 101 .

    The prime factor 101 must come from a single component,
    so that component has to be a chain supplying the factor 101,
    i.e. a chain with 100 elements of S.

    The factor 5 must also come from one component;
    the smallest way to obtain the factor 5 is a chain of length 4
    (containing 4 elements of S).

    The remaining factor 2³ = 8 is most economically provided by three
    isolated elements (each gives a factor 2).

5.  Putting the pieces together we obtain a set S consisting of  
      • 100 elements forming one long chain ( … S 0 S 0 … 0 S … ),  
      •   4 elements forming another (short) chain, and  
      •   3 isolated elements,

    altogether 100 + 4 + 3 = 107 elements.
    For this set the number of cousins is  
       101 · 5 · 2³ = 4040 ,
    exactly as required.

6.  It remains to show that no smaller set can have 4040 cousins.
    Every component with r elements of S contributes at most r+1
    (when it is a chain) or 2 (when it is an isolated element) possibilities;
    hence a set with fewer than 100 elements altogether can contribute at
    most  
       2¹⁰ · 11 = 2048 · 11 = 22528  (already far larger than needed)
    only if it already contained a chain of length at least 100,
    contradicting the assumption that |S|<100.
    Therefore at least 100 elements are necessary for the factor 101, and
    at least another 7 elements are needed to supply the remaining factor 40,
    so |S| ≥ 107.

Hence the least possible size of S is  

\boxed{107}.

Step 1.  Structure of a set that has cousins  
Let S be any finite set of integers that has at least one cousin T.  
• S cannot contain three consecutive integers, because the middle one would have no integer outside S that differs from it by 1.  
Thus every maximal block of consecutive elements of S has length 1 or 2.

Call  
A-block a single element of S (length 1)  
B-block two consecutive elements of S (length 2)

Step 2.  How many choices for T does a block give?  
Put a block by itself and count the possible partners in T.

A-block at x : the element x can be paired with x–1 or with x+1 → 2 possibilities.  
B-block {x, x+1}: x must pair with x–1 and x+1 must pair with x+2 → 1 possibility (forced).

Step 3.  When do different blocks interfere?  
Two A-blocks interfere only if they are exactly two units apart (⋯ x, x+2 ⋯); the two A-blocks together then have only 3, not 4, possibilities because they cannot both use the middle integer x+1.  
A string of n A-blocks, each two units apart, acts as one connected component and has n+1 possible partner sets (easy induction).

Therefore every connected component of S is of one of the following types.

• a single A-block – contributes a factor 2  
• a chain of n ( ≥ 2 ) A-blocks spaced exactly two apart – contributes a factor n+1  
• a component containing at least one B-block – contributes a factor 1 (all pairings forced)

Hence, if  
– c is the number of isolated A-blocks,  
– n1, n2, … are the lengths of the chains of A-blocks (ni ≥ 2),  
then

(number of cousins of S) = 2^c · Π (ni + 1).        (★)

Step 4.  What must S look like if it has 4040 cousins?  
4040 = 2^3 · 5 · 101.  
In (★) every factor other than a power of 2 arises solely from a chain of A-blocks, because isolated A-blocks give 2 and any component with a B-block gives 1.

• To get the prime factor 101 one chain must satisfy ni + 1 = 101 ⇒ ni = 100.  
  That chain contains 100 elements of S.

• To get the factor 5 we need another chain with ni + 1 = 5 ⇒ ni = 4.  
  That adds 4 more elements.

• The remaining factor 2^3 is most economically supplied by three isolated A-blocks  
  (each isolated A-block contributes a factor 2 while using only one element).

Therefore S must have at least  
100 (for 101) + 4 (for 5) + 3 (for 2^3) = 107 elements.

Step 5.  A set with 107 elements and exactly 4040 cousins  
Take  

C₁ = {0, 2, 4, … , 198}            (100 elements, a single chain → factor 101)  
C₂ = {300, 302, 304, 306}           (4 elements, chain → factor 5)  
C₃ = {400, 404, 408}                (3 isolated elements → factor 2^3)

Let S = C₁ ∪ C₂ ∪ C₃.  
The three parts are far apart, so their contributions are independent, and

#cousins(S) = 101 · 5 · 8 = 4040.

Thus a set with 107 elements exists, and from the lower-bound argument no smaller set can have 4040 cousins.

Final answer: \boxed{107}.

Let S be a finite set of integers and let

C(S)= the number of sets T that are cousins of S.

1.  Structure of a set that has cousins  

   • S can never contain three consecutive integers.  
   • All “blocks’’ of consecutive elements of S therefore have length 1 (a single
     integer) or 2 (a pair of neighbours).

   – A pair {k,k+1} is completely rigid: k must be matched with k−1 and k+1
     must be matched with k+2, so it contributes a factor 1 to C(S).

   – A single integer k may be matched with k−1 or k+1 (provided that choice
     is not already used elsewhere).  The only way two different elements of S
     can compete for the same neighbour is when the singletons occur every two
     units apart: …, s, _ , s, _ , s, …  (distance 2).  
     Such a string of m successive singletons 1 apart in S-coordinates

           s,   s+2,   s+4, … , s+2(m−1)

     together with their m+1 possible neighbours

           s−1, s+1, s+3, … , s+2m−1

     forms an isolated component.  A direct counting (or a short induction)
     shows that this component has exactly m+1 perfect matchings; i.e. it
     contributes the factor m+1 to C(S).

   Hence

        C(S)=∏(m_i+1),

   the product being taken over all the “strings’’ of singletons separated by 2.
   A block of two consecutive elements contributes nothing except its two
   elements, so to minimise |S| we simply omit such blocks.

2.  Translating the problem  

   Let the lengths of the singleton strings be m_1,m_2,…,m_k.  Then

          C(S)=∏(m_i+1)=4040

   and

          |S|=∑ m_i =∑(m_i+1)−k.

   We have to factor 4040 ( =2³·5·101 ) into integers ≥2 so that Σ(m_i+1)−k is
   as small as possible.

3.  The unavoidable 101  

   101 is prime, so every factorisation of 4040 has a factor 101.  Whatever else
   we do, that already contributes

          m =101−1=100

   elements to S.

4.  The remaining factor 40  

   We now minimise the number of elements coming from the remaining factor
   40.  List all multiplicative partitions of 40 and compute

          f_1,f_2,…  with  f_1·f_2·…=40 ,     cost = Σ(f_i−1).

   The best one is

          40 = 5·2·2·2

   because

          cost = (5−1)+(2−1)+(2−1)+(2−1)=4+1+1+1 = 7,

   and no other partition of 40 yields a smaller cost (one checks that any
   partition whose factors sum to 10 or less is impossible, so 7 is minimal).

5.  Total minimum size  

   Therefore

          |S|min  = 100  (from the 101-factor)
                     + 7 (from 5·2·2·2)  = 107.

6.  Exhibiting a set with 107 elements and 4040 cousins  

   Take five independent singleton strings:

        • 100 singletons:  1,3,5,…,199          ( length 100 → factor 101 )
        •   4 singletons: 1001,1003,1005,1007   ( length   4 → factor   5 )
        •   1 singleton:  2000                  ( factor 2 )
        •   1 singleton:  3000                  ( factor 2 )
        •   1 singleton:  4000                  ( factor 2 )

   These strings are separated by gaps >2, so their contributions multiply:

        C(S)=101·5·2·2·2 = 4040,

   and |S|=100+4+1+1+1=107.

Since 4040 cousins can be obtained with 107 elements and no smaller number of
elements can supply the necessary factor 101 together with the remaining
factor 40, the least possible size of S is

\boxed{107}.

Step 1.  Some structural facts about the sets  

For every positive integer n we have  
|A_n| = |A_n∩A_n| = gcd(n,n) = n,  
so each A_n contains exactly n elements.

If i | j then gcd(i , j)= i, hence  
|A_i∩A_j| = i = |A_i|,   
so A_i ⊆ A_j whenever i divides j.

Put  
B_n = A_n \bigcup_{d\mid n,\,d<n} A_d ;          (elements that appear for the first time in A_n)

The sets {B_n} are pairwise disjoint and
|B_n| = n − ∑_{d\mid n,\,d<n} |B_d|
        = n − ∑_{d\mid n,\,d<n} φ(d) = φ(n)             (well–known identity Σ_{d\mid n}φ(d)=n).

Thus there are exactly φ(n) “new’’ elements that are born in step n.

Step 2.  The eight relevant divisors of 250  

250 = 2·5³,  so the divisors we care about are  

1, 2, 5, 10, 25, 50, 125, 250.

Their Euler–phi values are  

φ(1)=1, φ(2)=1, φ(5)=4, φ(10)=4, φ(25)=20, φ(50)=20, φ(125)=100, φ(250)=100.

Step 3.  A lower bound for the required maxima  

List the elements of all A_n in increasing order.  
Let M_d be the largest element of A_d (the quantity whose sum we have to minimise).

•  d = 1  
  A₁ has one element, so M₁ ≥ 1.

•  d = 2  
  By the time we see M₂ we must have met
  B₁ (1 element) and B₂ (1 element).  
  Therefore M₂ ≥ 2.

•  d = 5  
  Before M₅ appears we must have met
  B₁(1) + B₂(1) + B₅(4) = 6 elements,  
  whence M₅ ≥ 6.

•  d = 10  
  Before M₁₀ appears we need
  B₁(1)+B₂(1)+B₅(4)+B₁₀(4)=10 elements ⇒ M₁₀ ≥ 10.

•  d = 25  
  We still need the four elements of B₁₀ and the single element of B₂, although neither
  10 nor 2 divides 25.
  Hence at least 5 “foreign’’ elements precede M₂₅, giving
  M₂₅ ≥ 25 + 5 = 30.

•  d = 50  
  All elements required so far belong to divisors of 50, so
  M₅₀ ≥ 50.

•  d = 125  
  The 1 + 4 + 20 = 25 elements in B₂, B₁₀ and B₅₀ are foreign to 125 yet must already
  be present, giving
  M₁₂₅ ≥ 125 + 25 = 150.

•  d = 250  
  Every one of the 250 elements must have appeared, so
  M₂₅₀ ≥ 250.

Consequently  
M₁ + M₂ + M₅ + M₁₀ + M₂₅ + M₅₀ + M₁₂₅ + M₂₅₀ ≥  
1 + 2 + 6 + 10 + 30 + 50 + 150 + 250 = 499.          (★)

Step 4.  A construction attaining the lower bound  

Create φ(n) distinct new integers for every divisor n of 250 and let B_n be this set.  
Now place the elements in the exact order

B₁ , B₂ , B₅ , B₁₀ , B₂₅ , B₅₀ , B₁₂₅ , B₂₅₀,

keeping the elements of each B_n in arbitrary order but making sure all
elements of the earlier B’s are smaller than every element of the later B’s.
Explicitly one possible sequence is

1                 (B₁)  
2                 (B₂)  
3 4 5 6           (B₅)  
7 8 9 10          (B₁₀)  
11 … 30           (20 numbers = B₂₅)  
31 … 50           (20 numbers = B₅₀)  
51 … 150          (100 numbers = B₁₂₅)  
151 … 250         (100 numbers = B₂₅₀).

For this arrangement we have  

M₁ = 1,  M₂ = 2,  M₅ = 6,  M₁₀ = 10,  M₂₅ = 30,  
M₅₀ = 50,  M₁₂₅ = 150,  M₂₅₀ = 250,

so the sum of the maxima is exactly 499, matching the lower bound (★).

Step 5.  Conclusion  

The minimum possible value of
      Σ_{d | 250}   max A_d
is therefore  

\boxed{499}

Step 1.  A convenient family of sets.  
For every positive integer t put  
E_t  =  φ(t) distinct elements,  
and let the sets of the sequence be  

A_i  =  ⋃_{t | i} E_t .                        (1)

Because the E_t ’s are pairwise disjoint we have  

|A_i ∩ A_j| = Σ_{t | gcd(i , j)} φ(t) = gcd(i , j) ,

so (1) satisfies the required intersection condition.  
Hence it is enough to decide how to assign actual positive integers to the eight
sets

E_1 , E_2 , E_5 , E_10 , E_25 , E_50 , E_125 , E_250                 (2)

whose cardinalities are  

φ(1)=1,  φ(2)=1,  φ(5)=4,  φ(10)=4,  φ(25)=20,  φ(50)=20,  φ(125)=100,  φ(250)=100 .

No other E_t can influence any A_d with d | 250, so only (2) matters.  
The total number of elements involved is 1+1+4+4+20+20+100+100 = 250.

Step 2.  What has to be minimised.  
Let M_t be the largest element placed in E_t.  
For every d | 250 the set A_d consists exactly of the E_t with t | d, so

max A_d = max_{t | d} M_t .                     (3)

Our target is therefore  

S = Σ_{d | 250} max A_d = Σ_{d | 250} max_{t | d} M_t .        (4)

Step 3.  Reducing the problem to a scheduling question.  
Because all the elements are distinct, the numbers 1,2,…,250 can be written
one after another, each number being put irreversibly into exactly one E_t.
Write the eight sets in the order in which their elements are completed; if
the sets appear in the sequence  

T_1 , T_2 , … , T_8

and p_{T_k}=|E_{T_k}|, then the kth completion time is  

C_k = p_{T_1}+p_{T_2}+⋯+p_{T_k} ,

and M_{T_k}=C_k.  
Hence (4) becomes  

S = Σ_{k=1}^{8} C_k .                           (5)

Minimising (5) is the classical single-machine scheduling problem
1|prec|∑C.  
Our precedence relations are “divides’’: set E_s must be finished before
E_t whenever s divides t.  The HORN-type interchange argument shows that
among the sets that are currently eligible (all their predecessors already
done) one should always finish the one with the smallest size first; any
other choice increases the sum of completion times.

Step 4.  The optimal order.  
Applying “shortest eligible first’’ gives the unique optimal
topological order

E_1 (1)  →  E_2 (1)  →  E_5 (4)  →  E_10 (4)  →  
E_25 (20) → E_50 (20) → E_125 (100) → E_250 (100),

where the numbers in parentheses are the sizes.

Step 5.  The completion times (hence the maxima).

C_1 = 1            M_1   = 1  
C_2 = 1+1   = 2    M_2   = 2  
C_3 = 2+4   = 6    M_5   = 6  
C_4 = 6+4   = 10   M_10  = 10  
C_5 = 10+20 = 30   M_25  = 30  
C_6 = 30+20 = 50   M_50  = 50  
C_7 = 50+100=150   M_125 = 150  
C_8 =150+100=250   M_250 = 250

Step 6.  The minimal value.

S_min = 1 + 2 + 6 + 10 + 30 + 50 + 150 + 250 = 499.

Therefore the smallest possible value of  
Σ_{d | 250} max A_d is  

\boxed{499}.

Step 1.  Basic consequences of the condition  
For every positive integers i, j
 |A_i∩A_j| = gcd(i,j).

•  Taking i=j gives |A_i|=i for every i.  
•  If j|i then gcd(i,j)=j, hence |A_i∩A_j|=|A_j|, so A_j⊆A_i whenever j|i.  
  Thus for any element x there is a unique minimal index m(x) and x belongs to all
  A_n with m(x)|n.

Step 2.  How many elements have each minimal index  
Let f(n)=#{x : m(x)=n}.  
For every positive n,
 n = |A_n| = Σ_{k|n} f(k).

By Möbius inversion  
 f(n)=φ(n), Euler’s totient of n.  
So for each divisor k of 250 we must have exactly φ(k) elements whose minimal
index equals k.

Step 3.  The divisors of 250 that matter  
250 = 2·5³,  Div(250)= {1,2,5,10,25,50,125,250}.  
For these eight indices we must realise the following totals

k : 1 2 5 10 25 50 125 250  
φ(k):1 1 4  4  20  20 100 100

Step 4.  Lower bounds for the maxima  
For each d|250 the set A_d contains exactly d elements, hence
 max A_d ≥ d.  
Often a larger lower bound is forced because elements coming from indices that
do not divide d cannot appear in A_d, so some of the smallest integers are
“blocked”.

Compute the minimum possible maximum M_d for every d.

•  d = 1 : needs 1 element → M₁ = 1.  
•  d = 2 : needs {A₁,A₂} ⇒ {1, ?}.  1 is used, so 2 is the next
 smallest allowed number → M₂ = 2 (cannot be smaller).  
•  d = 5  
 Numbers ≤5 available to A₅ are 1,3,4,5 (since 2 belongs to A₂ and A₂⊈A₅).  
 Only 4 numbers; A₅ needs 5.  Therefore one number ≥6 is necessary → M₅ ≥ 6.  
•  d = 10  
 Among 1…9 only 9 numbers are available but A₁₀ needs 10 → M₁₀ ≥ 10.  
•  d = 25  
 Within 1…29 there are 5 numbers already belonging to A₂ or A₁₀, so only
 24 remain for A₂₅ which needs 25 elements → M₂₅ ≥ 30.  
•  d = 50  
 All numbers ≤50 except those in A₁₂₅ are allowed, but the latter start
 after 50, so M₅₀ ≥ 50.  
•  d = 125  
 Up to 150 there are exactly 25 numbers forced into A₂ and A₁₀ or A₅₀,
 leaving 125 available for A₁₂₅ – just enough.  Hence M₁₂₅ ≥ 150.  
•  d = 250  
 Needs 250 different integers, so M₂₅₀ ≥ 250.

Step 5.  Construction attaining all these minima  
Choose the integers exactly so that every lower bound above is met.

Group (minimal index)  assigned labels  
G₁ (φ=1)        {1}  
G₂ (φ=1)        {2}  
G₅ (φ=4)        {3,4,5,6}  
G₁₀ (φ=4)       {7,8,9,10}  
G₂₅ (φ=20)       {11,…,30}  
G₅₀ (φ=20)       {31,…,50}  
G₁₂₅ (φ=100)      {51,…,150}  
G₂₅₀ (φ=100)      {151,…,250}

Now for every divisor d of 250 the set A_d is the union of all G_k with k|d.
The corresponding maxima are

M₁ = 1  
M₂ = 2  
M₅ = 6  
M₁₀ = 10  
M₂₅ = 30  
M₅₀ = 50  
M₁₂₅ = 150  
M₂₅₀ = 250

Each attains its proven lower bound, therefore they are the minimum possible.

Step 6.  Required sum  
Σ_{d|250} M_d = 1 + 2 + 6 + 10 + 30 + 50 + 150 + 250 = 499.

Hence the minimum possible value is  

\boxed{499}

Step 1.  Basic consequences of the intersection condition  
Because |A_i∩A_j| = gcd(i,j), taking i = j gives |A_i| = i.  
If j | i then gcd(i,j) = j = |A_j|, so A_j ⊂ A_i.  
Hence whenever j divides i, A_j is a subset of A_i.

Step 2.  Building the sets recursively  
Put A₁ = {1}.  
For n > 1 let B_n be a set of new elements, disjoint from all earlier ones, and define  

 A_n =  ⋃_{d|n,\,d<n} A_d  ∪  B_n.  

Because of the nesting property this construction is forced: every element that first appears in B_n will afterwards live in every A_k with n | k, and in no other sets.  Let b_n = |B_n|.

Step 3.  Determining |B_n|  
For every n,

 n = |A_n| = Σ_{d|n} b_d.  

By Möbius inversion,

 b_n = Σ_{d|n} μ(d)·(n/d) = φ(n),

where μ is the Möbius function and φ is Euler’s totient.  
Thus exactly φ(n) new elements are introduced at step n.

Step 4.  The largest element of A_n  
Introduce the new elements in increasing numerical order.  
After processing the integers 1,2,…,n we have used exactly

 S(n) = Σ_{k=1}^n φ(k)

different positive integers, and they all lie in A_n; none larger has been used yet.  
Hence

 max A_n = S(n).

Step 5.  Values of S(n) for the divisors of 250  
Compute the running totient sum:

 S(1)   =   1  
 S(2)   =   2  
 S(5)   =  10  
 S(10)  =  32  
 S(25)  = 200  
 S(50)  = 774  
 S(125) = 4796  
 S(250) = 19024

Step 6.  Required sum  
Add the eight values corresponding to the divisors of 250:

 1 + 2 + 10 + 32 + 200 + 774 + 4796 + 19024 = 24839.

Therefore the minimum possible value of  Σ_{d|250} max A_d  is

\boxed{24839}.

Step 1.  A convenient family of sets  
For every positive integer t create φ(t) distinct elements ( φ is Euler’s totient).  
Put each of these elements into every A_n with t | n.

Because  

 • |{t : t | n}| = Σ_{t | n} φ(t) = n,  

 • Σ_{t | i , t | j} φ(t) = gcd(i , j),  

this construction gives |A_n| = n and |A_i ∩ A_j| = gcd(i , j) for all i , j, so the required family of sets exists.  
For our problem we only have to look at the elements whose “label’’ t divides 250, because an element with t ∤ 250 is in none of the sets A_d ( d | 250 ).

Step 2.  The elements that matter  
The eight divisors of 250 = 2·5³ are  

 1, 2, 5, 10, 25, 50, 125, 250.  

For them the numbers of corresponding elements are

 φ(1)=1, φ(2)=1, φ(5)=4, φ(10)=4, φ(25)=20,  
 φ(50)=20, φ(125)=100, φ(250)=100,

altogether 250 elements.

Step 3.  A candidate assignment  
Give these 250 elements the integers 1,…,250 in the order of increasing t:

 t = 1 : 1        ( max₁ = 1)  
 t = 2 : 2        ( max₂ = 2)  
 t = 5 : 3-6       ( max₅ = 6)  
 t = 10 : 7-10      ( max₁₀ = 10)  
 t = 25 : 11-30      ( max₂₅ = 30)  
 t = 50 : 31-50      ( max₅₀ = 50)  
 t = 125 : 51-150     ( max₁₂₅ = 150)  
 t = 250 : 151-250    ( max₂₅₀ = 250)

Hence  
max A₁ = 1,  
max A₂ = 2,  
max A₅ = 6,  
max A₁₀ = 10,  
max A₂₅ = 30,  
max A₅₀ = 50,  
max A₁₂₅ = 150,  
max A₂₅₀ = 250,

and

Σ_{d | 250} max A_d = 1 + 2 + 6 + 10 + 30 + 50 + 150 + 250 = 499.

Step 4.  Why 499 is minimal  
We now show that no arrangement can give a smaller sum.

1.  A₁ has one element, so max A₁ ≥ 1.

2.  A₂ must contain two different elements, therefore max A₂ ≥ 2.

3.  A₅ must contain the element of A₁ and four elements that are not in A₂  
(because |A₂ ∩ A₅| = 1).  
These 4 new elements have to be at least 3,4,5,6, so max A₅ ≥ 6.

4.  Altogether 9 distinct numbers are < 10, but A₁₀ needs 10 elements, hence max A₁₀ ≥ 10.

5.  Among the first 29 numbers there are at most 29 – 25 = 4 positions not usable by A₂₅ elements.  
Yet we already need 1 element from A₂ (t = 2) and 4 elements from t = 10, i.e. 5 elements that cannot lie in A₂₅.  
So at least one A₂₅–element is forced to position 30 or higher, giving max A₂₅ ≥ 30.

6.  All 50 elements that divide 50 must appear in A₅₀, so max A₅₀ ≥ 50.

7.  There are 25 elements whose labels do not divide 125 (t = 2,10,50).  
Thus among the first 149 positions at most 124 can be occupied by elements of A₁₂₅; this is not enough, so max A₁₂₅ ≥ 150.

8.  A₂₅₀ has 250 elements, so max A₂₅₀ ≥ 250.

Adding these unavoidable lower bounds we get

min Σ_{d | 250} max A_d ≥ 1 + 2 + 6 + 10 + 30 + 50 + 150 + 250 = 499.

Step 5.  Conclusion  
The arrangement constructed in Step 3 attains this lower bound, therefore 499 is the minimum possible value of the required sum.

\boxed{499}

Step 1.  How many elements must each set contain?  
Put  

c_d = number of elements that belong to every A_n with d | n and to none of the others.

Then for every positive integer g

|A_i∩A_j| = ∑_{d|gcd(i,j)} c_d = gcd(i,j).

Moebius inversion gives c_d = φ(d).  
Hence, for every n

|A_n| = ∑_{d|n} φ(d) = n.

So each A_n must contain exactly n elements and, for the divisors of 250,

|A_1|=1, |A_2|=2, |A_5|=5, |A_{10}|=10, |A_{25}|=25,
|A_{50}|=50, |A_{125}|=125, |A_{250}|=250.

Step 2.  Which types of elements occur in these eight sets?  
Only the elements of types d dividing 250 matter.  
Their multiplicities are  

type 1 : 1 element  
type 2 : 1 element  
type 5 : 4 elements  
type 10 : 4 elements  
type 25 : 20 elements  
type 50 : 20 elements  
type 125 : 100 elements  
type 250 : 100 elements

(the numbers are φ(d)).

Step 3.  A lower bound for the maxima.

•  A_1 needs one element, so M_1 ≥ 1.  
•  A_2 needs two elements, so M_2 ≥ 2.  
•  A_5 must contain its 5 own elements plus the element of type 2 that is needed earlier, so at least 6 different numbers are forced: M_5 ≥ 6.  
•  A_10 already needs 10 distinct elements, so M_10 ≥ 10.  
•  A_25 must contain its 25 own elements; before it is finished the element of type 2 and the four elements of type 10 have already been used, so at least 30 distinct numbers are required: M_25 ≥ 30.  
•  A_50 can be finished with its 50 elements if no type 125 element appears before that, hence M_50 ≥ 50.  
•  A_125 must contain its 125 own elements, and before it is finished the 1+4+20 = 25 elements of types 2, 10, 50 have been used, so M_125 ≥ 150.  
•  A_250 needs 250 elements, hence M_250 ≥ 250.

Thus  
M_1+M_2+M_5+M_{10}+M_{25}+M_{50}+M_{125}+M_{250} ≥  
1+2+6+10+30+50+150+250 = 499.       (★)

Step 4.  An arrangement that attains the bound.

Label the 250 relevant elements with the integers 1,…,250 in this order

1 : the unique element of type 1  
2 : the element of type 2  
3–6 : the four elements of type 5  
7–10 : the four elements of type 10  
11–30 : the twenty elements of type 25  
31–50 : the twenty elements of type 50  
51–150 : the hundred elements of type 125  
151–250 : the hundred elements of type 250.

With this labelling

M_1 = 1,   M_2 = 2,   M_5 = 6,   M_{10} = 10,  
M_{25} = 30,   M_{50} = 50,   M_{125} = 150,   M_{250} = 250,

and the sum of the eight maxima equals the lower bound (★).

Step 5.  Conclusion.

Because 499 is a lower bound and is attained, it is the minimum possible value.

\boxed{499}

Step 1.  A workable model for the sets  

For every positive integer t create φ(t) (Euler totient) distinct “atoms’’
e(t,1), … , e(t,φ(t)).
Put an atom e(t,*) into A_n iff t divides n.

For any n

|A_n| = Σ_{t|n} φ(t) = n,

and for any i,j

|A_i ∩ A_j| = Σ_{t|gcd(i,j)} φ(t) = gcd(i,j).

Hence this construction fulfils the required condition, and, because |A_n|=n
is the minimum possible, it is optimal with respect to set–sizes.  
Only the labels we attach to the atoms (distinct positive integers) can still
affect Σ_{d|250} max A_d.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 2.  Which atoms appear in the eight relevant sets?

An atom e(t,*) lies in A_d exactly when t|d.
Therefore a label matters for the eight sets
d∈{1,2,5,10,25,50,125,250} iff t divides 250.
The relevant values of t and their totients are

t =  1 (φ=  1)   frequency in the eight sets = 8  
t =  2 (φ=  1)   frequency = 4  
t =  5 (φ=  4)   frequency = 6  
t = 10 (φ=  4)   frequency = 3  
t = 25 (φ= 20)   frequency = 4  
t = 50 (φ= 20)   frequency = 2  
t =125 (φ=100)   frequency = 2  
t =250 (φ=100)   frequency = 1  

Altogether there are 250 atoms:

frequency 8 : 1 atom  
frequency 6 : 4 atoms  
frequency 4 : 21 atoms  
frequency 3 : 4 atoms  
frequency 2 : 120 atoms  
frequency 1 : 100 atoms  

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 3.  Turning the problem into a rearrangement problem  

Let f(e) be the frequency (1–8) of an atom e in the eight sets and
let ℓ(e) be its label.
For every k, define
F(k)=number of sets whose maximum label is at least k.
One checks that

Σ_{d|250} max A_d = Σ_{k≥1} F(k)
                  = Σ_e f(e)·ℓ(e).

Thus the objective is a weighted sum of the labels, the weight being the
frequency.  To minimise it we should attach the smallest labels to the
largest weights (rearrangement inequality).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 4.  Optimal assignment of the labels 1,…,250  

Give the labels in increasing order to atoms
listed by non-increasing frequency:

label 1         →  the single atom of frequency 8  
labels 2–5      →  the four atoms of frequency 6  
labels 6–26     →  the twenty-one atoms of frequency 4  
labels 27–30    →  the four atoms of frequency 3  
labels 31–150   →  the 120 atoms of frequency 2  
labels 151–250  →  the 100 atoms of frequency 1  

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 5.  Computing the minimal possible sum  

S = Σ f(e)·ℓ(e)

= 8·1
  + 6·(2+3+4+5)
  + 4·(6+7+…+26)
  + 3·(27+28+29+30)
  + 2·(31+…+150)
  + 1·(151+…+250)

= 8
  + 6·14
  + 4·336
  + 3·114
  + 2·10 860
  + 1·20 050

= 8 + 84 + 1344 + 342 + 21 720 + 20 050

= 43 548.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 6.  Conclusion  

The arrangement above is feasible and achieves the lower bound given by the
rearrangement inequality, so 43 548 is the minimum possible value.

\boxed{43548}

Step 1.  A convenient description of all possible families  

Define disjoint blocks \(B_1 ,B_2 ,B_3 ,\dots\) of (so far unlabeled) elements and put  

\[
A_n=\bigcup_{d\mid n} B_d .
\]

Let \(b_d=|B_d|\).  
Then  

\[
|A_n|=\sum_{d\mid n} b_d ,\qquad  
|A_i\cap A_j|=\sum_{d\mid\gcd(i,j)} b_d .
\]

Because the problem demands \(|A_n|=n\) and
\(|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j)\) for all \(i,j\), we need  

\[
\sum_{d\mid n} b_d = n\qquad\text{for every }n\ge 1 .
\tag{1}
\]

By Möbius inversion  

\[
b_n=\sum_{d\mid n}\mu(d)\frac{n}{d}=\varphi(n),
\]

i.e. the blocks must have cardinalities \(|B_n|=\varphi(n)\) (Euler’s
totient) and choosing the family
\(A_n=\bigcup_{d\mid n}B_d\) with those sizes indeed satisfies all
requirements.  We are free to decide which positive integers belong to
each block.

For the task we need only the blocks whose indices divide \(250\):
 \(1,2,5,10,25,50,125,250\).
Their sizes are

\[
\varphi(1)=1,\; \varphi(2)=1,\; \varphi(5)=4,\; \varphi(10)=4,\;
\varphi(25)=20,\; \varphi(50)=20,\; \varphi(125)=100,\;
\varphi(250)=100,
\]

adding up to \(250\) elements in all.

Step 2.  Inevitable lower bounds for the required maxima  

Denote by \(\mathrm{max}(d)\) the largest element of \(A_d\).

a)  \(A_1\) contains one element, so \(\mathrm{max}(1)\ge 1\).

b)  \(A_2=A_1\cup B_2\) needs two elements, so
\(\mathrm{max}(2)\ge 2\).

c)  \(A_5=A_1\cup B_5\) has \(1+4=5\) elements.
Since \(A_2\) must be contained in the first two positions and
\(B_2\not\subseteq A_5\), the four elements of \(B_5\) cannot fit into
positions \(2\)–\(5\); consequently \(\mathrm{max}(5)\ge 6\).

d)  \(A_{10}=A_1\cup B_2\cup B_5\cup B_{10}\) uses \(10\) elements, so
\(\mathrm{max}(10)\ge 10\).
All positions \(1\)–\(10\) must be filled by blocks that divide \(10\);
hence no element of \(B_{25}\) may occur before position \(11\).

e)  \(A_{25}=A_1\cup B_5\cup B_{25}\) needs \(20\) elements from
\(B_{25}\).  
Because the first \(10\) positions are already used up by \(A_{10}\),
only \(15\) positions (\(11\)–\(25\)) are left, too few for the \(20\)
elements of \(B_{25}\).  Therefore those must spread at least to
position \(30\): \(\mathrm{max}(25)\ge 30\).

f)  \(A_{50}=A_1\cup B_2\cup B_5\cup B_{10}\cup B_{25}\cup B_{50}\)
needs exactly \(50\) elements, so \(\mathrm{max}(50)\ge 50\) and again
no element of \(B_{125}\) may precede position \(51\).

g)  \(A_{125}=A_1\cup B_5\cup B_{25}\cup B_{125}\) requires the
\(100\) elements of \(B_{125}\).  Only \(75\) positions
(\(51\)–\(125\)) are free, hence \(B_{125}\) must extend to
position \(150\):
\(\mathrm{max}(125)\ge 150\).

h)  \(A_{250}\) contains every block and therefore needs \(250\)
positions, so \(\mathrm{max}(250)\ge 250\).

Thus the unavoidable lower bounds are  

\[
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{max}(1)&\ge 1,&
\mathrm{max}(2)&\ge 2,&
\mathrm{max}(5)&\ge 6,&
\mathrm{max}(10)&\ge 10,\\
\mathrm{max}(25)&\ge 30,&
\mathrm{max}(50)&\ge 50,&
\mathrm{max}(125)&\ge 150,&
\mathrm{max}(250)&\ge 250.
\end{aligned}
\]

Their sum is \(499\).  Hence the desired total cannot be smaller than
\(499\).

Step 3.  A construction attaining all lower bounds  

Assign the actual positive integers to the blocks as follows

\[
\begin{aligned}
B_1 &=\{1\},\\
B_2 &=\{2\},\\
B_5 &=\{3,4,5,6\},\\
B_{10}&=\{7,8,9,10\},\\
B_{25}&=\{11,12,\dots ,30\},\\
B_{50}&=\{31,32,\dots ,50\},\\
B_{125}&=\{51,52,\dots ,150\},\\
B_{250}&=\{151,152,\dots ,250\}.
\end{aligned}
\]

Because each \(A_n\) is the union of the blocks dividing \(n\), one now
calculates

\[
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{max}(1)&=1,\\
\mathrm{max}(2)&=2,\\
\mathrm{max}(5)&=6,\\
\mathrm{max}(10)&=10,\\
\mathrm{max}(25)&=30,\\
\mathrm{max}(50)&=50,\\
\mathrm{max}(125)&=150,\\
\mathrm{max}(250)&=250,
\end{aligned}
\]

exactly the lower bounds found above.  Therefore the sum over all
divisors \(d\mid 250\) equals

\[
1+2+6+10+30+50+150+250 = 499.
\]

Step 4.  Minimality

Since \(499\) is both a lower bound and is attained by the explicit
family above, it is the minimum possible value.

\boxed{499}
