Let the ordered sequence of upper faces during the six rolls be  
(T₁ , T₂ , T₃ , T₄ , T₅ , T₆), each entry being one of the six faces.

1.  Conditions for all even stickers (2, 4, 6) to be visible  
   • The faces receiving stickers 2, 4, 6 are pairwise distinct; denote them by x, y, z:  
     T₂ = x , T₄ = y , T₆ = z with x≠y≠z.  
   • After sticker 2 is placed, that face is never rolled again:  
     T₃ , T₄ , T₅ , T₆ ≠ x.  
   • After sticker 4 is placed, that face is never rolled again:  
     T₅ , T₆ ≠ y.  

   Hence  
     T₁ : any of 6 faces  
     T₃ : any of the 5 faces ≠ x  
     T₅ : any of the 4 faces ≠ x,y.

   For each ordered triple (x,y,z) there are 6·5·4 = 120 sequences, and there are 6·5·4 = 120 ways to choose (x,y,z).  
   Total sequences satisfying “even stickers visible”:  
     D = 120 · 120 = 14 400.

2.  Add the requirement “exactly one blank face”  
   This means exactly five distinct faces appear in the whole sequence, i.e. besides x,y,z we need exactly two more distinct faces and must not see the sixth face.

   The three faces not among {x,y,z} are labelled A, B, C.  
   Exactly two of them, say A and B, must appear (C must not).

   • Choose the pair {A,B}: C(3,2) = 3 ways.  
   • Positions T₁ , T₃ , T₅ must be chosen from {A,B,x,y,z}, must contain both A and B, and must respect  
        T₃ ≠ x , T₅ ≠ x,y.  
     Allowed options per position:  
        T₁ : 5 choices (A,B,x,y,z)  
        T₃ : 4 choices (A,B,y,z)  
        T₅ : 3 choices (A,B,z)

     Total triples without further restriction: 5·4·3 = 60.  
     Exclude triples missing A (4·3·2 = 24), missing B (another 24), add back those missing both (3·2·1 = 6).  
     Valid triples = 60 – 24 – 24 + 6 = 18.

   Thus, for each (x,y,z): 3 · 18 = 54 valid ways for (T₁ , T₃ , T₅).  
   With 120 choices of (x,y,z) we get  
     N = 120 · 54 = 6 480 sequences satisfying both conditions.

3.  Conditional probability  
     p = N / D = 6 480 / 14 400 = 9 / 20.

Hence p = 9/20 = m/n with m+n = 9+20 = 29.

Let the ordered 6-tuple  
(t₁,t₂,t₃,t₄,t₅,t₆)  
denote the faces that show on the successive rolls when stickers 1,2,…,6 are placed.  
Each tᵢ is chosen independently and uniformly from the 6 faces, so there are 6⁶ total possible sequences.

1.  Interpreting “visible”.  
   The sticker that is visible on a face is the one with the largest number ever placed on that face.  
   Thus sticker k is visible iff no sticker with a higher number is later placed on the same face.

2.  Conditions for the event E ( “2,4,6 are visible”).  
   • t₂ , t₄ and t₆ must be on three distinct faces.  
   • No later index may reuse a face already occupied by a visible even sticker:  

     t₃,t₄,t₅,t₆ ≠ t₂ , t₅,t₆ ≠ t₄ .

   Aside from these, no other restrictions exist.

3.  Counting sequences satisfying E.  
   Choose faces for t₂ , t₄ , t₆ : 6·5·4 = 120 ways.  
   Then

        t₁ : any face………………6 choices  
        t₃ : any face ≠ t₂………5 choices  
        t₅ : any face ≠ t₂ ,t₄…4 choices  

   Hence  

        N_E = 120·6·5·4 = 14 400.

4.  Distinct faces and blank faces.  
   A face is blank at the end iff it never appears among t₁,…,t₆.  
   Having exactly one blank face is equivalent to the sequence using exactly 5 distinct faces.

5.  Counting sequences in E that use exactly 5 distinct faces.  
   With the three distinct faces S = {t₂ ,t₄ ,t₆} fixed, let R be the other three faces.  
   We must add exactly two new faces from R via t₁ ,t₃ ,t₅, leaving one face W ∈ R never used.

   Fix W (3 choices).  
   Allowed sets for the three positions once W is forbidden are  

        |t₁| = 5 , |t₃| = 4 , |t₅| = 3  (5·4·3 = 60 sequences).

   Let U,V be the two remaining faces of R.  
   We need both U and V to appear at least once among t₁ ,t₃ ,t₅.

   Using inclusion–exclusion:

        sequences with U absent: (4)(3)(2) = 24  
        sequences with V absent: 24  
        sequences with both U & V absent: (3)(2)(1) = 6

        sequences with both U and V present:
            60 − 24 − 24 + 6 = 18.

   Therefore, for each choice of the blank face W there are 18 good sequences; for the three possible W, 3·18 = 54.

   Hence, for each triple (t₂ ,t₄ ,t₆) there are 54 sequences producing exactly one blank face.

   Total good sequences:  

        N_good = 120 · 54 = 6 480.

6.  Conditional probability.

        p = N_good / N_E = 6 480 / 14 400
          = 9 / 20.

Thus p = 9/20, so m + n = 9 + 20 = 29.

\boxed{29}

Step 1.  Description of the process  
After the six rolls each face of the die is

• blank if it never appeared,  
• showing the number t if it last appeared on roll t.

Thus a sticker t is visible iff the face rolled on turn t is never rolled again later.

Step 2.  Probability that the even stickers are visible  
Let Xi be the face rolled on turn i.  
We need

(i) X2 ≠ X3,X4,X5,X6 and  
(ii) X4 ≠ X5,X6.

Generate X3–X6 successively, keeping X2 fixed:

P(X3 ≠ X2)=5/6  
P(X4 ≠ X2)=5/6  
P(X5 ≠ X2,X4)=4/6=2/3  
P(X6 ≠ X2,X4)=4/6=2/3

Therefore  
P(E) = (5/6)(5/6)(2/3)(2/3)=25/81.

Step 3.  Characterising “exactly one blank face’’  
Having exactly one blank means that among the six rolls

• exactly 5 distinct faces appear,  
• one face (call it D) appears twice and the other four appear once.

Let F0 be the missing (blank) face.  There are 6 choices for F0 and 5 for D.

Step 4.  Counting sequences that satisfy both conditions

Fix F0 and D.

Case 1: X2 = D  
The second D must be at position 1 (otherwise X2 would be covered later).  
Positions 3,4,5,6 receive the four single faces in any order.  
Count: 4! = 24.

Case 2: X2 ≠ D  
The two D’s may be in any pair of positions chosen from {1,3,4,5,6} except  
{4,5} and {4,6} (otherwise X4 = X5 or X6).  
From the 10 possible pairs we exclude these 2, leaving 8 pairs.  
The four single faces (one of which goes to position 2) can be placed in 4! ways.  
Count: 8·4! = 8·24 = 192.

Total for fixed F0,D: 24+192 = 216.

With 6·5 = 30 possible (F0,D) pairs,

Number of sequences with both properties  
N(B ∧ E) = 30·216 = 6480.

Step 5.  Conditional probability

Total sequences: 6^6 = 46656  
Sequences with E only: 46656·25/81 = 14400.

Hence  
p = N(B ∧ E)/N(E) = 6480/14400 = 9/20.

Step 6.  Final answer  
p = 9/20 = m/n with m+n = 9+20 = 29.

Let the six rolls of the die produce the ordered sequence of faces  

(j₁ , j₂ , j₃ , j₄ , j₅ , j₆),

where sticker k is placed on face j_k (faces are numbered 1–6 and each roll is independent and uniform).

1.  Characterising event E  
   “Stickers 2, 4 and 6 are visible”

   • j₂ , j₄ , j₆ are all on different faces.  
   • For sticker 2 to stay visible: j₃ , j₄ , j₅ , j₆ ≠ j₂.  
   • For sticker 4 to stay visible: j₅ , j₆ ≠ j₄.  

   From this we have the following choices:

        j₂ : 6 possibilities
        j₃ : 5  (≠ j₂)
        j₄ : 5  (≠ j₂)
        j₅ : 4  (≠ j₂ , j₄)
        j₆ : 4  (≠ j₂ , j₄)
        j₁ : 6  (no restriction)

   Total sequences satisfying E:

        N(E) = 6·5·5·4·4·6 = 14 400.

2.  Characterising event A  
   “Exactly one face is still blank”  
   ⇔ exactly 5 distinct faces appear in the six rolls.

3.  Counting sequences that satisfy both A and E  

   Choose the one blank face first: 6 possibilities.

   Now all six rolls must land on the remaining 5 faces, and, among those 5 faces, every one must appear at least once.  
   Denote by T₅ the number of sequences on a fixed set of 5 faces that satisfy the visibility restrictions of step 1.

   For n available faces the number of sequences satisfying the visibility restrictions is

        n² (n−1)² (n−2)²
        (j₂: n   ; j₃: n−1 ; j₄: n−1 ;
         j₅: n−2 ; j₆: n−2 ; j₁: n )

   Hence  
        T₅ = 5²·4²·3² = 3600.

   We need the sequences that actually use all 5 faces, so apply inclusion–exclusion over the 5 faces:

        total on 5 faces            :  3600
        − sequences missing 1 face  :  −5·(4²·3²·2²)   = −5·576  = −2880
        + sequences missing 2 faces :  +10·(3²·2²·1²)  = +10·36  =  +360
        (missing 3 or more faces is impossible under the restrictions)

        S = 3600 − 2880 + 360 = 1080.

   Therefore  

        N(A ∧ E) = 6 (choice of blank face) · 1080 = 6480.

4.  Conditional probability

        p = P(A | E) = N(A ∧ E) / N(E)
          = 6480 / 14 400
          = 9 / 20.

Thus  

        p = 9/20 ⇒ m = 9 , n = 20 , m + n = 29.

r₁ : any of the 5 faces in S₀ ∪ T\{c} → 5 choices

For the fixed triple (r₂ , r₄ , r₆) there are 3 choices for which face of T is the blank, so

18 · 3 = 54

triples (r₁ , r₃ , r₅) give both A and B.

Thus the total sequences for A ∧ B are

120 (choices for r₂ , r₄ , r₆) · 54 = 6480.

P(A) = 14400 / 6⁶,  P(A ∧ B) = 6480 / 6⁶.

p = P(B | A) = P(A ∧ B) / P(A) = 6480 / 14400 = 9 / 20.

\boxed{29}.

Let the six rolls of the die be the ordered sequence  

(s₁ , s₂ , s₃ , s₄ , s₅ , s₆), sᵢ ∈ {1,2,3,4,5,6}.

After all six stickers are placed, sticker k is visible exactly when no later sticker was ever put on the same physical face.

Event E ( “all even stickers are visible” ) means  
• s₂ ≠ s₃ ,s₄ ,s₅ ,s₆ (sticker 2 un-covered)  
• s₄ ≠ s₅ ,s₆      (sticker 4 un-covered)

Sticker 6 is always visible because it is the last one placed.

1.  Counting sequences that satisfy E  

For any fixed value of s₂, each of s₃,s₄,s₅,s₆ must avoid it: factor (5/6)^4.  
Given s₂, s₄ must also avoid s₂ (5/6 factor) and s₅,s₆ must avoid s₄ (4/6 each).  
Counting directly,

number of possibilities for (s₂,…,s₆) = 5·5·4·4 = 400  
(5 choices for s₃, 5 for s₄, 4 for s₅, 4 for s₆),

so total sequences satisfying E:

6 choices for s₂  ⇒ 6·400 = 2400 choices for (s₂,…,s₆)  
6 arbitrary choices for s₁ ⇒ 2400·6 = 14400 sequences.

Hence  
P(E) = 14400 / 6⁶ = 14400 / 46656 = 25/81.

2.  Adding the requirement “exactly one blank face” (event A)

Exactly one face of the die is never rolled ⇔ exactly five distinct faces appear in the six rolls.  
Thus, among the six positions there is precisely one repeated face (multiplicity pattern 2,1,1,1,1).

Let the duplicated positions be (i , j) with i < j.

Because of E:

• Any pair (2 , j) with j > 2 is forbidden (that would cover sticker 2).  
• Pairs (4 ,5) and (4 ,6) are forbidden (that would cover sticker 4).  
• Pair (2 ,4) is also impossible (s₄ ≠ s₂ is required).

The 15 position–pairs reduce to the following 9 admissible pairs:

(1,2) , (1,3) , (1,4) , (1,5) , (1,6) , (3,4) , (3,5) , (3,6) , (5,6).

For any admissible pair:

• Choose the duplicated face f – 6 possibilities.  
• Choose 4 distinct faces from the remaining 5 and assign them to the remaining 4 positions –  
  P(5,4)=5·4·3·2 = 120 possibilities.  
• Every such assignment automatically satisfies all the E–constraints (s₃..s₆ ≠ s₂ and s₅,s₆ ≠ s₄) and gives exactly one blank face.

Thus each pair contributes 6·120 = 720 sequences, and

number of sequences satisfying A ∩ E = 9 · 720 = 6480.

Therefore  

P(A ∩ E) = 6480 / 6⁶ = 6480 / 46656 = 5 / 36.

3.  Conditional probability

p = P(A | E) = P(A ∩ E) / P(E) = (5/36) ÷ (25/81) = 9 / 20.

So p = 9/20, and with p = m/n in lowest terms,

m + n = 9 + 20 = 29.

We are given  

(1) a² + ab + b² = 3(a + b)  
(2) a² + ac + c² = 3(a + c)  
(3) b² + bc + c² = 5(b + c) + 1  

with a, b, c pairwise distinct.  
--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Subtract (1) from (2):

 (a² + ac + c²) − (a² + ab + b²) − 3(a + c − a − b) = 0  

 (ac − ab) + (c² − b²) − 3(c − b) = 0  

 a(c − b) + (c − b)(c + b) − 3(c − b) = 0  

Since b ≠ c, divide by (c − b):

 a + (c + b) − 3 = 0  ⇒ a = 3 − (b + c). (4)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Let s = b + c.  
   Substitute a = 3 − s into equation (1):

 (3 − s)² + (3 − s)b + b² = 3[(3 − s) + b].

 Expanding and simplifying gives  

 (b + c)² − b(b + c) + b² = 3(b + c)

 ⇒ b² + bc + c² = 3(b + c). (5)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Compare (5) with the given (3):

 b² + bc + c² = 5(b + c) + 1.

Set the right–hand sides equal:

 3(b + c) = 5(b + c) + 1

 ⇒ 2(b + c) + 1 = 0  

 ⇒ b + c = −1/2. (6)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Insert (6) into (4):

 a = 3 − (b + c) = 3 − (−1/2) = 3 + 1/2 = 7/2.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5.  Existence check  

With s = −1/2, equation (5) becomes b² + bc + c² = −3/2, which indeed has (complex, distinct) solutions for b and c. Thus no contradiction arises, and the value of a is uniquely determined.

 a² + ac + c² – a – 3c – 7 = 0.  (7)

4.  Subtract (6) from (7):

  x² + ½x + 7/4 = 0  ⇒  4x² + 2x + 7 = 0,

   whose discriminant is −108, ensuring b and c are distinct and different from a, satisfying all three original equations.

Hence the required value of a is

\boxed{\dfrac{7}{2}}

From  
1) a² + ab + b² = 3(a + b)  
2) a² + ac + c² = 3(a + c)  
3) b² + bc + c² = 5(b + c) + 1

--------------------------------------------------------
Step 1:  Subtract (1) from (2):
 a(c – b) + (c² – b²) – 3(c – b) = 0  
 (c – b)[a + (c + b) – 3] = 0.

Since b ≠ c, we must have  
a + b + c = 3. (∗)

--------------------------------------------------------
Step 2:  Rewrite (1) and (2) as quadratics in b and c.

(1) b² + (a – 3)b + a(a – 3) = 0  
(2) c² + (a – 3)c + a(a – 3) = 0.

Thus b and c are the two (distinct) roots of  
x² + (a – 3)x + a(a – 3) = 0,

giving  
b + c = 3 – a, bc = a(a – 3). (†)  
(The first identity agrees with (∗).)

--------------------------------------------------------
Step 3:  Use (3).

Write the left side of (3) as  
b² + bc + c² = (b + c)² – bc.

Substitute (†):

(b + c)² – bc = 5(b + c) + 1  
(3 – a)² – a(a – 3) = 5(3 – a) + 1.

Compute:

(a² – 6a + 9) – (a² – 3a) = 15 – 5a + 1  
–3a + 9 = 16 – 5a  
2a = 7  
a = 7/2.

--------------------------------------------------------
Step 4:  Distinctness check.

With a = 7/2,  
b + c = –1/2, bc = 7/4.  
The discriminant for b, c is  
(b + c)² – 4bc = (–1/2)² – 7 = –27/4 ≠ 0,

so b and c are distinct and different from a.  
Hence all conditions are satisfied.

--------------------------------------------------------
Final value:
\boxed{\dfrac{7}{2}}

Step 1 –  Rewrite each given equation in terms of symmetric sums  

1) a² + ab + b² = 3(a + b)  
2) a² + ac + c² = 3(a + c)  
3) b² + bc + c² = 5(b + c) + 1  

Recall the identity x² + xy + y² = (x + y)² – xy.  
Let  

S₁ = a + b, P₁ = ab  
S₂ = a + c, P₂ = ac  
S₃ = b + c, P₃ = bc  

Then  

(1) S₁² – P₁ = 3S₁ ⟹ P₁ = S₁² – 3S₁  
(2) S₂² – P₂ = 3S₂ ⟹ P₂ = S₂² – 3S₂  
(3) S₃² – P₃ = 5S₃ + 1 ⟹ P₃ = S₃² – 5S₃ – 1  

Step 2 –  Obtain the sum a + b + c  

Subtract (2) from (1):  

(ab – ac) + (b² – c²) = 3(b – c)  
(b – c)[a + (b + c)] = 3(b – c)  

Since b ≠ c, divide by (b – c):  

a + b + c = 3.  (4)

Step 3 –  Express b + c and bc in terms of a  

From (4): b + c = 3 – a.  (5)

Equations (1) and (2) are identical in the variable form, so both b and c satisfy  

x² + (a – 3)x + (a² – 3a) = 0.  (6)

Hence  
b + c = –(a – 3) = 3 – a  (consistency with (5))  
bc   = a² – 3a.  (7)

Thus  
S₃ = 3 – a, P₃ = a² – 3a.

Step 4 –  Use equation (3) with these expressions  

Left-hand side of (3):  
b² + bc + c² = S₃² – P₃  
        = (3 – a)² – (a² – 3a)  
        = 9 – 6a + a² – a² + 3a  
        = 9 – 3a.  

Right-hand side of (3):  
5(b + c) + 1 = 5(3 – a) + 1 = 16 – 5a.  

Set them equal: 9 – 3a = 16 – 5a  
        2a = 7  
        a = 7/2.

Step 5 –  Verify distinctness (optional)  

With a = 7/2, polynomial (6) becomes  

x² + ½x + 7/4 = 0 ⟹ 4x² + 2x + 7 = 0,  

whose discriminant is –108 < 0, giving two non-real roots  
b = (–1 + 3i√3)/4, c = (–1 – 3i√3)/4,  

distinct from each other and from a = 7/2, fulfilling the conditions.

Final answer:  
\boxed{\dfrac{7}{2}}

First write the three given relations with everything on the left–hand side:

(1) a² + ab + b² − 3a − 3b = 0  
(2) a² + ac + c² − 3a − 3c = 0  
(3) b² + bc + c² − 5b − 5c − 1 = 0  

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Obtain the common sum a + b + c

Subtract (2) from (1):

(a² + ab + b² − 3a − 3b) − (a² + ac + c² − 3a − 3c)  
= ab − ac + b² − c² − 3b + 3c  
= (b − c)(a + b + c − 3) = 0.

Since the numbers are pairwise distinct, b ≠ c, hence

a + b + c = 3.  (4)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Express b in terms of a and c and substitute

From (4): b = 3 − a − c.

Insert this into equations (1) and (3).

•  Equation (1):

a² − 3a + ac + c² − 3c = 0.  (5)

•  Equation (3):

a² − a + ac + c² − 3c − 7 = 0.  (6)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Eliminate c to find a

Subtract (5) from (6):

(a² − a + ac + c² − 3c − 7) − (a² − 3a + ac + c² − 3c) = 0  
⇒ −a + 3a − 7 = 0  
⇒ 2a − 7 = 0  
⇒ a = 7/2.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  (Consistency check)

With a = 7/2, equation (5) becomes

c² + (1/2)c + 7/4 = 0 ⇒ 4c² + 2c + 7 = 0,

which indeed has (distinct) complex solutions  
c = (−1 ± 3i√3)/4, giving corresponding distinct b.  
Thus the obtained value of a is valid.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Final answer

\boxed{\dfrac{7}{2}}

Step 1.  Express the series with divisor function  
For n ≥ 1

 1∕(10ⁿ–1)=10^{-n}+10^{-2n}+10^{-3n}+⋯ ,

so  
S = Σ_{n=1}^{∞} 1/(10ⁿ–1) = Σ_{n=1}^{∞} Σ_{k=1}^{∞} 10^{-nk}.

Each power 10^{-m} appears once for every pair (n,k) with nk=m, i.e. once for every divisor of m.  
Hence

 S = Σ_{m=1}^{∞} d(m)·10^{-m},                       (1)

where d(m) is the number of positive divisors of m.

Step 2.  Multiply by 10^{100}  
From (1)

 10^{100}S = Σ_{m=1}^{∞} d(m)·10^{100-m}.            (2)

Write the sum as two parts:

 A = Σ_{m=1}^{100} d(m)·10^{100-m}      (integer part from m≤100)  
 B = Σ_{m=101}^{∞} d(m)·10^{100-m}     (fractional tail).

Step 3.  The tail contributes no integer  
For m>100 set r = m–100 (r≥1):

 B = Σ_{r=1}^{∞} d(100+r)·10^{-r}.

The first term is d(101)·10^{-1}=2·0.1=0.2.  
Since d(n) ≤ n,  

 B < Σ_{r=1}^{∞} (100+r)·10^{-r} = 0.2+0.08+0.002+⋯ < 0.283 < 1.

Therefore ⌊B⌋ = 0; the tail supplies no integer part (and no carry).

Step 4.  Last three digits come from m = 98, 99, 100 only  
In A each summand is d(m)·10^{100-m}.  
Because 10^{k} ≡ 0 (mod 1000) for k≥3, only the exponents

 100–m = 2,1,0  (i.e. m = 98, 99, 100)

affect the remainder modulo 1000.  All terms with m≤97 are multiples of 1000, hence irrelevant.

Compute the needed divisor counts:

 98 = 2·7² ⇒ d(98) = (1+1)(2+1) = 6  
 99 = 3²·11 ⇒ d(99) = (2+1)(1+1) = 6  
 100 = 2²·5² ⇒ d(100) = (2+1)(2+1) = 9

So

 Contribution of m=98:  d(98)·10^{2} = 6·100 = 600  
 Contribution of m=99:  d(99)·10^{1} = 6·10  =  60  
 Contribution of m=100: d(100)·10^{0} = 9·1   =   9

Adding them: 600 + 60 + 9 = 669.

Since (i) no other term alters the last three digits, and   
(ii) the tail B supplies no integer (Step 3), we have

 ⌊10^{100}S⌋ ≡ 669  (mod 1000).

Step 5.  Final answer  
The remainder when ⌊10^{100}S⌋ is divided by 1000 is therefore

\boxed{669}.

Then  

  S = Σ_{n=1}^{∞} Σ_{m=1}^{∞} 10^{-mn}
    = Σ_{k=1}^{∞} d(k) 10^{-k},

where d(k) is the number of positive divisors of k (because k=mn can be written in d(k) ways).

Thus the decimal expansion of S is obtained from the sequence d(1),d(2),d(3),… with the usual base-10 carries performed from right to left.

For k ≥ 201,

  d(k) ≤ k  so  Σ_{k=201}^{∞} d(k)10^{-k} < Σ_{k=201}^{∞} k10^{-k}
                  < 1.25·201·10^{-201} ≈ 2.5·10^{-199} < 10^{-100}.

After all carries, the digits at positions 98, 99 and 100 (counting the first digit after the decimal point as position 1) are

First rewrite the series in a form that makes the power of \(10\) explicit.

   \]
   Summing over \(k\ge 1\),
   \[
   S=\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}\frac1{10^{k}-1}
     =\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}\;\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}10^{-kn}.
   \]

2.  Swap the order of summation.  
    For each positive integer \(m\) the term \(10^{-m}\) appears once for every ordered factorisation \(m=kn\); the number of such pairs is the divisor function \(d(m)\). Hence
    \[
    S=\sum_{m=1}^{\infty} d(m)\,10^{-m}.
    \]

3.  Multiply by \(10^{100}\):
   \[
   10^{100}S=\sum_{m=1}^{\infty} d(m)\,10^{100-m}
            =\underbrace{\sum_{m=1}^{100} d(m)\,10^{100-m}}_{\text{integer}}
             +\underbrace{\sum_{m=101}^{\infty} d(m)\,10^{100-m}}_{<\;1/3}.
   \]
   The tail is less than
   \[
   \sum_{m=101}^{\infty} 2\cdot10^{100-m}
      =2\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}10^{-n}= \tfrac{2}{9}<\tfrac13,
   \]
   so the fractional part of \(10^{100}S\) is strictly between \(0\) and \(1\).  
   Therefore
   \[
   \Bigl\lfloor 10^{100}S\Bigr\rfloor
     =\sum_{m=1}^{100} d(m)\,10^{100-m}.
   \]

4.  Only the last three powers of \(10\) affect the value modulo \(1000\).  
   These come from \(m=98,99,100\):
   \[
   10^{100-98}=10^{2}=100,\quad
   10^{100-99}=10^{1}=10,\quad
   10^{100-100}=10^{0}=1.
   \]

   • \(98 = 2\cdot7^{2}\)  ⇒ \(d(98)=(1+1)(2+1)=6\)  
   • \(99 = 3^{2}\cdot11\) ⇒ \(d(99)=(2+1)(1+1)=6\)  
   • \(100 = 2^{2}\cdot5^{2}\) ⇒ \(d(100)=(2+1)(2+1)=9\)

6.  Compute the contribution modulo \(1000\):
   \[
   100\cdot d(98)+10\cdot d(99)+d(100)
   =100\cdot6 + 10\cdot6 + 9
   =600 + 60 + 9
   =669\pmod{1000}.
   \]

Hence the remainder when \(\lfloor10^{100}S\rfloor\) is divided by \(1000\) is

\boxed{669}.

98 = 2·7^2 ⇒ τ(98) = (1+1)(2+1) = 6  
99 = 3^2·11 ⇒ τ(99) = (2+1)(1+1) = 6  
100 = 2^2·5^2 ⇒ τ(100) = (2+1)(2+1) = 9

Since each of these values is < 10, no internal carries arise among the
98th–100th digits, so the reduction above is exact.

Therefore  

A (mod 1000) = 6·10^{2} + 6·10^{1} + 9·10^{0} = 600 + 60 + 9 = 669

Hence the remainder when ⌊10^{100} S⌋ is divided by 1000 is

\boxed{669}

Let  

S = Σ_{n=1}^{∞} 1/(10^{n}-1) = 1/9 + 1/99 + 1/999 + …  

We need R, the remainder when ⎣10^{100} S⎦ is divided by 1000.

1. Express S with the divisor function.

For n ≥ 1,

1/(10^{n}-1) = Σ_{k=1}^{∞} 10^{-nk}.

Hence

S = Σ_{n=1}^{∞} Σ_{k=1}^{∞} 10^{-nk}  
  = Σ_{m=1}^{∞} d(m) 10^{-m},

where d(m) is the number of positive divisors of m (because nk = m).

2. Multiply by 10^{100}:

10^{100} S = Σ_{m=1}^{∞} d(m) 10^{100-m}.

Write this as A = B + C with

B = Σ_{m=1}^{100} d(m) 10^{100-m}  (an integer)  
C = Σ_{m=101}^{∞} d(m) 10^{100-m}  (a fractional tail).

Because 10^{100-m} ≤ 10^{-1} for m ≥ 101 and d(m) is small (d(101)=2, d(102)=8, …), one finds

C < 0.3  ⇒  ⎣C⎦ = 0.

Thus ⎣10^{100} S⎦ = B, and we only need B mod 1000.

3. Reduce B mod 1000.

For m ≤ 97, 100-m ≥ 3, so 10^{100-m} is a multiple of 1000 and contributes 0 modulo 1000.  
Thus only m = 98, 99, 100 matter:

m = 98: 10^{100-98} = 10^{2} = 100 ⇒ term = d(98)·100  
m = 99: 10^{100-99} = 10^{1} = 10  ⇒ term = d(99)·10  
m = 100: 10^{100-100} = 1         ⇒ term = d(100)

Compute the divisor counts:
d(98)  = d(2·7²)     = (1+1)(2+1) = 6  
d(99)  = d(3²·11)    = (2+1)(1+1) = 6  
d(100) = d(2²·5²)    = (2+1)(2+1) = 9  

Now

d(98)·100 = 6·100 = 600  
d(99)·10  = 6·10  = 60  
d(100)    = 9

Sum modulo 1000: 600 + 60 + 9 = 669.

4. The fractional part C adds nothing to the integer part, so

⎣10^{100} S⎦ ≡ 669 (mod 1000).

\boxed{669}

Write the n-th term of the series in a geometric-series form:

1/(10^n-1)=10^{-n}/(1-10^{-n})=\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}10^{-kn}

Hence  

S=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac1{10^n-1}=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}10^{-kn}
   =\sum_{m=1}^{\infty}d(m)\,10^{-m},

where d(m) denotes the number of positive divisors of m (because each pair (k,n) with kn=m contributes one 10^{-m}).  

Set  

T=10^{100}S=\sum_{m=1}^{\infty}d(m)10^{100-m}  
  =\underbrace{\sum_{m=1}^{100}d(m)10^{100-m}}_{\text{integer part}}
   +\underbrace{\sum_{m=101}^{\infty}d(m)10^{100-m}}_{F\;(\text{fractional part})}.

1.  Size of the fractional part  
   For m≥101 let r=m-100≥1.  Then

   F=\sum_{r=1}^{\infty}d(100+r)10^{-r}
     <\sum_{r=1}^{\infty}30\cdot10^{-r}=\frac{30}{9}\;=\;3.\!3\overline{3}<10.

   (In fact the first few terms give F≈0.283, but the loose bound 10 is already
   enough.)  So F<10; in particular F<1, hence it cannot increase the integer
   part of T.  Therefore
   floor(T)=∑_{m=1}^{100}d(m)10^{100-m}.

2.  Digits that matter for the last three decimal places  
   Only m=98,99,100 contribute to the 10^2,10^1,10^0 places respectively, and
   any carry can come only from lower places.

   • m=100→10^{0}-place: d(100)=9  
   • m=99 →10^{1}-place: d(99)=6  
   • m=98 →10^{2}-place: d(98)=6  

   The units digit is 9, so no carry is produced.  
   The tens digit is 6, still <10, so again no carry.  
   Hence the hundreds digit remains 6.

   Therefore the last three digits of floor(T) are 6 6 9.

So the required remainder is

boxed{669}.

Let  

S = Σ_{n=1}^{∞} 1/(10^{n} – 1).

We need  
⌊10^{100} S⌋ (mod 1000).

1.  Write  
T = 10^{100} S = Σ_{n=1}^{∞} 10^{100}/(10^{n}-1).

   For n ≥ 101, 10^{100} < 10^{n}-1, so each of those terms is < 1 and contributes
   nothing to the integer part.  Their total is  

   Σ_{n=101}^{∞} 10^{100}/(10^{n}-1) < Σ_{k=1}^{∞} 1/10^{k} = 1/9 < 1,  

   so no carry comes from this tail.  Hence  

   ⌊10^{100}S⌋ = Σ_{n=1}^{100} Q_n,      with   Q_n = ⌊10^{100}/(10^{n}-1)⌋.

2.  Compute each Q_n modulo 1000.

   Let 100 = qn + r, 0 ≤ r < n.  
   Then 10^{100} = 10^{r}(10^{n})^{q} = 10^{r}[(10^{n}-1)+1]^{q}.  
   Expanding shows  

   Q_n = 10^{r} (1 + 10^{n} + 10^{2n} + … + 10^{(q-1)n}).

   Since we only need the result modulo 1000 and 10^{3} ≡ 0 (mod 1000),
   a power 10^{k} contributes iff k ≤ 2.

   • n = 1  
     q = 100, r = 0 → Q₁ ≡ 1 + 10 + 100 = 111  (mod 1000).

   • n = 2  
     q = 50, r = 0 → Q₂ ≡ 1 + 100 = 101  (mod 1000).

   • n ≥ 3  
     Only the term with i = 0 can have exponent r + 0 ≤ 2.  
     Therefore Q_n ≡ 10^{r} (mod 1000):

        r = 0  ⇒ 10^{r} ≡ 1  
        r = 1  ⇒ 10^{r} ≡ 10  
        r = 2  ⇒ 10^{r} ≡ 100  
        r ≥ 3 ⇒ 0.

     Hence Q_n’s contribution depends solely on r = 100 mod n:

        r = 0  ⇔  n | 100, n ≥ 3   → 7 such n: 4,5,10,20,25,50,100
        r = 1  ⇔  n | 99 , n ≥ 3   → 5 such n: 3,9,11,33,99
        r = 2  ⇔  n | 98 , n ≥ 3   → 4 such n: 7,14,49,98

     Sum of their residues:

        (7 × 1) + (5 × 10) + (4 × 100) = 7 + 50 + 400 = 457.

3.  Add all contributions (mod 1000):

   Q₁ + Q₂ + Σ_{n=3}^{100} Q_n ≡ 111 + 101 + 457 ≡ 669  (mod 1000).

4.  No extra carry (tail < 1), so

   ⌊10^{100} S⌋ ≡ 669  (mod 1000).

\boxed{669}

Write the series in a more convenient form.

1.  Each denominator is a string of k nines, i.e. 10^k−1, so  

   S = Σ_{k=1}^{∞} 1/(10^k−1).

2.  Expand every term as a geometric series:

   1/(10^k−1) = Σ_{n=1}^{∞} 10^{−kn}.

   Thus  
   S = Σ_{k=1}^{∞} Σ_{n=1}^{∞} 10^{−kn}
     = Σ_{m=1}^{∞} d(m)·10^{−m},  

   where d(m) is the number of positive divisors of m (because m=kn can be factored into k·n in d(m) ways).

3.  Multiply by 10^{100}:

   10^{100}S = Σ_{m=1}^{∞} d(m)·10^{100−m}
             = P + F,

   with   
   P = Σ_{m=1}^{100} d(m)·10^{100−m}      (an integer)  
   F = Σ_{m=101}^{∞} d(m)·10^{100−m}     (a positive fraction).

   Only the terms m=98, 99, 100 can influence the last three digits of P, because 10^{100−m} is ≥10^3 for m≤97 and therefore contributes 000 mod 1000.

   d(98) = 6   (98 = 2·7²)  
   d(99) = 6   (99 = 3²·11)  
   d(100)= 9   (100 = 2²·5²)

   Contributions to P:

   m = 98 : 6·10² = 600  
   m = 99 : 6·10¹ =  60  
   m =100 : 9·10⁰ =   9  
   -----------------------  
   P ≡ 600+60+9 ≡ 669  (mod 1000).

4.  Bound F to see whether it can raise the integer part:

   F = Σ_{k=1}^{∞} d(100+k)·10^{−k}.  
   Since d(n) is at most about 20 in this range and 10^{−k} decays rapidly,

       F < 0.2 + 0.08 + 0.002 + 0.0008 + … < 0.29.

   Hence 0 < F < 1 and floor(10^{100}S) = P.

5.  Therefore the greatest integer ≤10^{100}S is congruent to 669 mod 1000.

\boxed{669}

Write each term as a geometric series:

1/(10^k−1)=1/10^k·1/(1−10^{−k})=∑_{m=1}^{∞}10^{−km}.

Hence  
S = ∑_{k=1}^{∞}∑_{m=1}^{∞}10^{−km}  
  = ∑_{n=1}^{∞}τ(n)10^{−n},

because τ(n) (the divisor function) counts the pairs (k,m) with km=n.

Multiplying by 10^{100} gives  

10^{100}S = ∑_{n=1}^{∞}τ(n)10^{100−n}.

The integer part ⌊10^{100}S⌋ contains only the terms with 1≤n≤100; terms with n>100 contribute a value strictly between 0 and 1.

Thus  
N = ⌊10^{100}S⌋ = ∑_{n=1}^{100}τ(n)10^{100−n}.

To find N mod 1000 we need only those addends where the factor 10^{100−n} is 1, 10 or 100, i.e. where 100−n = 0,1,2 ⇒ n = 100,99,98.

Compute τ for these n:

• 100 = 2²·5²  → τ(100) = (2+1)(2+1) = 9  
•  99 = 3²·11   → τ(99)  = (2+1)(1+1) = 6  
•  98 = 2·7²    → τ(98)  = (1+1)(2+1) = 6

Their contributions to N:

n =100: 9·10^{0}   = 9  
n = 99: 6·10^{1}   = 60  
n = 98: 6·10^{2}   = 600  
Sum                   = 669.

All other terms are multiples of 10^{3} and hence contribute 0 modulo 1000.

Therefore  
N ≡ 669 (mod 1000).

\boxed{669}

Let  

S = Σ_{k=1}^{∞} 1/(10^k – 1)  
T = 10^{100} S .

We must find ⌊T⌋ (mod 1000).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Re–express the sum S

For any k ≥ 1  

1/(10^k – 1)=10^{-k}+10^{-2k}+10^{-3k}+··· .  

Hence  

S = Σ_{k=1}^{∞} Σ_{m=1}^{∞} 10^{-km}
  = Σ_{n=1}^{∞} τ(n) ·10^{-n},                        (†)

because a term 10^{-n} appears once for every ordered pair (k,m) with km=n;  
the number of such pairs is the divisor–counting function τ(n).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Shift the decimal point 100 places

Multiplying (†) by 10^{100} gives  

T = 10^{100} S = Σ_{n=1}^{∞} τ(n)·10^{100-n}.  

Write the exponent as E = 100 – n (so n = 100 – E).  
Then all terms with n ≥ 101 have E ≤ –1 and are purely fractional;  
all integer powers come from 1 ≤ n ≤ 100 ( i.e. 0 ≤ E ≤ 99 ):

⌊T⌋ = Σ_{E=0}^{99} τ(100 – E) ·10^{E}.             (‡)

Thus the base-10 digits of ⌊T⌋ are obtained, right-to-left, from  
τ(100), τ(99), τ(98), … , τ(1), after the usual carrying.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Digits in the units, tens and hundreds places

We only need the last three digits, so we examine

E = 0 (d = 100): τ(100) = 9  
E = 1 (d = 99) : τ(99)  = 6  
E = 2 (d = 98) : τ(98)  = 6  

Because each τ(n) ≤ 12 for n ≤ 100, any carry coming from the units
place can be at most 1, and that in turn can make the carry into the
hundreds place at most 1.  Here τ(100)=9 produces no carry at all, so
every subsequent carry is 0.

Therefore  

units digit  = 9  
tens digit   = 6  
hundreds digit = 6

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Conclusion

The remainder when ⌊10^{100} S⌋ is divided by 1000 is

\boxed{669}.

Write the sum in a form that lets us see the first 100 decimal “places”.

1. Expand each term:
   1/(10^n – 1)= (1/10^n)/(1 – 10^{–n})  
                = (1/10^n) Σ_{m=0}^{∞} 10^{–nm}  
                = Σ_{m=1}^{∞} 10^{–nm}.

2. Add over n:
   S = Σ_{n=1}^{∞} Σ_{m=1}^{∞} 10^{–nm}
     = Σ_{k=1}^{∞} (number of ordered pairs (n,m) with nm=k) ·10^{–k}
     = Σ_{k=1}^{∞} d(k) ·10^{–k},          d(k) = number of divisors of k.

3. Multiply by 10^{100}:
   10^{100}S = Σ_{k=1}^{∞} d(k)·10^{100–k}.

   For k>100 the exponent is negative, so those terms are <1 and lie entirely in the fractional part.  
   Hence

   ⌊10^{100}S⌋ = Σ_{k=1}^{100} d(k)·10^{100–k}.

4. To find this integer mod 1000 we only need the contributions of k=98,99,100 because
   10^{100–k} ≡ 0 (mod 1000) whenever k ≤ 97 (since 100–k ≥ 3).

   So,
   remainder R = d(98)·10^{2} + d(99)·10^{1} + d(100)·10^{0}  (mod 1000).

5. Calculate the needed divisor counts:
   • 98 = 2·7²  ⇒ d(98) = (1+1)(2+1) = 6  
   • 99 = 3²·11 ⇒ d(99) = (2+1)(1+1) = 6  
   • 100 = 2²·5² ⇒ d(100) = (2+1)(2+1) = 9

6. Compute R:
   R = 6·100 + 6·10 + 9 = 600 + 60 + 9 = 669.

Therefore the remainder when ⌊10^{100}S⌋ is divided by 1000 is

\boxed{669}.

Rewrite each term of the series as a geometric series:

1/(10^n−1)=1/10^n⋅1/(1−10^{−n})=∑_{k=1}^{\infty}10^{−nk}.

Hence

S=∑_{n=1}^{\infty}∑_{k=1}^{\infty}10^{−nk}
  =∑_{m=1}^{\infty}τ(m)·10^{−m},

where τ(m) is the number of positive divisors of m (because nk=m exactly when n is a divisor of m).

Multiply by 10^{100}:

10^{100}S = ∑_{m=1}^{\infty} τ(m)·10^{100-m}
          = A + F,

with A = ∑_{m=1}^{100} τ(m)·10^{100-m} (integer part) and  
F = ∑_{m=101}^{\infty} τ(m)·10^{100-m} (fractional part).

Bounding the fractional part: for m>100 we have 10^{100-m} ≤ 10^{-1}.  Since τ(m) is at most a few hundred in this range, the first term is ≤0.2, the second ≤0.08, and the whole tail forms a rapidly decreasing geometric-type series whose sum is <0.3.  Thus 0<F<1 and floor(10^{100}S)=A.

We only need A modulo 1000.  Observe 10^{e}≡0 (mod 1000) for e≥3, while 10^{0}≡1, 10^{1}≡10, 10^{2}≡100.  Hence, in
A = ∑_{m=1}^{100} τ(m)·10^{100-m},
all terms vanish mod 1000 except those with 100−m = 0,1,2; i.e. m = 100, 99, 98.

Compute the needed divisor counts:
τ(100) = τ(2^2·5^2) = (2+1)(2+1) = 9  
τ(99)  = τ(3^2·11)   = (2+1)(1+1) = 6  
τ(98)  = τ(2·7^2)    = (1+1)(2+1) = 6

Therefore
A (mod 1000) ≡ τ(100)·1 + τ(99)·10 + τ(98)·100
             ≡ 9 + 6·10 + 6·100
             ≡ 9 + 60 + 600
             ≡ 669   (mod 1000).

Since the fractional part adds no additional whole unit, floor(10^{100}S)≡669 (mod 1000).

\boxed{669}

Let  

S = Σ_{k=1}^{∞} 1/(10^k – 1) = 0.s₁s₂s₃ …  ( sₙ are the decimal digits ).

Multiplying by 10¹⁰⁰ gives  

10¹⁰⁰ S = 0.s₁s₂ … s₁₀₀ s₁₀₁ … ,  

so the integer part ⌊10¹⁰⁰ S⌋ is simply the 100-digit number s₁ s₂ … s₁₀₀.  
We are asked for that number modulo 1000, i.e. for its last three digits.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Writing the nth digit of S

The term 1/(10^k – 1) has the repeating block consisting of k – 1 zeros followed
by a 1, so in the decimal expansion of S a 1 appears in every position that is
a multiple of k.  Therefore, before carrying is performed,

sₙ = τ(n)  (the number of positive divisors of n).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Carrying and a modular observation

When we actually add the columns, carries travel to the left.
For the first 100 digits it suffices to know what happens when we
multiply the series by 10¹⁰⁰; any term with k ≥ 101 then contributes
less than 10^{100}/(10^{101}–1) < 0.1, and the whole tail k ≥ 101
adds less than 1/9 ≈ 0.111… .
Hence the tail cannot change the integer part, so we may truncate the
series at k = 100:

N = ⌊10¹⁰⁰ S⌋ = ⌊ Σ_{k=1}^{100} 10¹⁰⁰ /(10^k – 1) ⌋ .

For each denominator m = 10^k – 1 (k ≤ 100) perform division:

10¹⁰⁰ = Q_k m + R_k , 0 ≤ R_k < m ,

so that  

10¹⁰⁰ /(10^k – 1) = Q_k + R_k /(10^k – 1).

Because 10^k ≡ 1 (mod m), we have  

R_k = 10^{100 mod k} = 10^r , r = 100 mod k (with 10^0 = 1).

Thus

Q_k = (10¹⁰⁰ – 10^r)/(10^k – 1)
     = 10^r (1 + 10^k + 10^{2k} + … + 10^{k(n–1)}),

where n = ⌊100/k⌋.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Qₖ modulo 1000

Because 10³ ≡ 0 (mod 1000), any power 10^e with e ≥ 3 vanishes modulo 1000.
Hence Q_k (mod 1000) only receives contributions from terms whose exponents
r + jk are 0, 1 or 2.  This happens only if r = 0, 1 or 2, i.e. when

r = 0  ⟺ k | 100,
r = 1  ⟺ k | 99  and k ≥ 3,
r = 2  ⟺ k | 98  and k ≥ 7.

Compute Q_k (mod 1000):

• r = 0, k = 1 → exponents 0,1,2 contribute: 1 + 10 + 100 = 111  
• r = 0, k = 2 → exponents 0,2 contribute: 1 + 100 = 101  
• r = 0, k ≥ 3 → only exponent 0 contributes: 1  

  (k = 4,5,10,20,25,50,100 : 7 of them)

• r = 1 (k = 3,9,11,33,99) → only exponent 1 contributes: 10 each

• r = 2 (k = 7,14,49,98) → only exponent 2 contributes: 100 each

Adding these residues:

111  (k = 1)
+101 (k = 2)
+  7 (seven 1’s)
+ 50 (five 10’s)
+400 (four 100’s)
——————————
669  (mod 1000)

So  

Σ_{k=1}^{100} Q_k ≡ 669  (mod 1000).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  The fractional parts

The fractional remainder from each k is

F_k = R_k /(10^k – 1) = 10^{r-k} / (1 – 10^{-k})   (< 0.112 · 10^{r-k}).

Because k – r ≥ 1, we have 10^{r-k} ≤ 10^{-1}.
Hence F_k < 0.012 for k ≥ 2, while F_1 = 1/9 ≈ 0.111.
Therefore  

0 < Σ_{k=1}^{100} F_k < 0.111 + 99·0.012 < 0.3 < 1.

The tail k ≥ 101 contributes less than 0.111 as noted earlier.
Consequently the complete fractional part is still < 1, so no additional
carry is produced.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5.  Result

Thus

⌊10¹⁰⁰ S⌋ ≡ Σ_{k=1}^{100} Q_k ≡ 669  (mod 1000).

The required remainder is

\boxed{669}.

1.  Expand each term:  
   1/(10ⁿ−1) = (1/10ⁿ)/(1−10⁻ⁿ) = ∑_{k=1}^{∞}10^{-nk}.  

2.  Therefore  
   S = ∑_{n=1}^{∞}1/(10ⁿ−1)   
     = ∑_{n=1}^{∞}∑_{k=1}^{∞}10^{-nk}  
     = ∑_{d=1}^{∞}τ(d)·10^{-d},  
   where τ(d) is the number of positive divisors of d (each pair (n,k) with nk=d contributes one 10^{-d}).

3.  Multiply by 10¹⁰⁰:  
   10¹⁰⁰S = ∑_{d=1}^{∞}τ(d)·10^{100-d}.  
   Split this into an “integer part’’ (d≤100) and a “fractional part’’ (d≥101).

4.  Only the terms with 100−d = 0,1,2 affect the last three digits of the integer part because 10³≡0 (mod 1000).  
   These come from d = 100, 99 and 98:

   τ(100)=9   (100 = 2²·5²)  
   τ(99)=6    (99  = 3²·11)  
   τ(98)=6    (98  = 2·7²)

   Hence, modulo 1000,
   A = τ(100)·10⁰ + τ(99)·10¹ + τ(98)·10²  
     = 9 + 6·10 + 6·100  
     = 669  (mod 1000).

5.  The fractional part is  
   B = ∑_{k=1}^{∞}τ(100+k)·10^{-k}.  
   Compute just enough terms to see its size:

      k   100+k   τ(100+k)   contribution
      1     101       2         0.2
      2     102       8         0.08
      3     103       2         0.002
      4     104       8         0.0008
      5     105       8         0.00008
      6     106       4         0.000004
      7     107       2         0.0000002
      8     108      12         0.00000012
      9     109       2         0.000000002
     10     110       8         0.0000000008

   Summing these ten contributions gives ≈ 0.282884323.  
   Each remaining term is at most τ(n)·10^{-k} ≤ 1000·10^{-11}/(1−0.1) < 1.2×10^{-8}.  
   Therefore B < 0.283 and, in particular, 0 ≤ B < 1, so floor(B)=0; there is no carry into the integer part.

6.  The greatest integer ≤10¹⁰⁰S is therefore exactly A, whose residue modulo 1000 is 669.

m=100 : 9   (units)  
m=99  : 6   (tens)

Step 1.  Rewrite the series in terms of its decimal digits  
For every positive integer n,

  1/(10ⁿ–1)=0.\overline{0…01}   (the repeating block has one ‘1’ followed by n–1 zeros).

Hence, at the k-th place after the decimal point the digit of 1/(10ⁿ–1) is 1 exactly when n|k and 0 otherwise.  
Therefore the k-th digit of the sum

  S=1/9+1/99+1/999+⋯

equals the number of positive divisors of k, denoted d(k).  Thus

  S=∑_{k≥1} d(k)·10^{-k}.                         (1)

Step 2.  Shift the decimal point 100 places  
Multiplying (1) by 10^{100} gives

  10^{100}S = ∑_{k=1}^{100} d(k)·10^{100-k} + R,

where the “tail”

  R = ∑_{k≥101} d(k)·10^{100-k}

contains all the fractional part.  Because d(k)≤k,

  R < ∑_{k=101}^\infty k·10^{100-k}
      < ∑_{m=1}^\infty (100+m)·10^{-m}
      < 0.1/(1–0.1)+0.1/(1–0.1) = 0.111…+0.111…≈0.112.

Hence 0≤R<0.112; in particular R is too small to raise the integer part by 1.

Step 3.  Find the three least–significant digits of the integer part  
Only the terms with powers 10^{0},10^{1},10^{2} matter for the remainder modulo 1000, i.e. k=100,99,98.

 k=100: d(100)=9 → contribution 9·10^{0}=9 (units place)  
 k= 99: d(99)=6 → contribution 6·10^{1}=60 (tens place)  
 k= 98: d(98)=6 → contribution 6·10^{2}=600 (hundreds place)

Thus, before carrying, the last three digits are 6 6 9.

Step 4.  Check that no additional carries occur  
• d(100)=9<10, so no carry goes from the units to the tens.  
• d(99)=6 and receives no carry, so no carry goes to the hundreds.  
• R<0.112 adds less than 1 to the total, so it cannot change the units digit.

Therefore the digits 6 6 9 are final.

Step 5.  Conclude the required remainder  
The greatest integer ≤10^{100}S leaves a remainder of 669 upon division by 1000.

\boxed{669}

Write each denominator as 10^k – 1:

S = Σ_{k=1}^{∞} 1/(10^{k}-1).

Expand each term as a geometric series:

1/(10^{k}-1) = 10^{-k}(1 + 10^{-k} + 10^{-2k} + …).

Collecting the coefficient of 10^{-n} shows that it equals d(n), the number of positive divisors of n:

S = Σ_{n=1}^{∞} d(n)·10^{-n}.

Multiply by 10^{100}:

10^{100}S = Σ_{n=1}^{∞} d(n)·10^{100-n}
          = Σ_{n=1}^{100} d(n)·10^{100-n} + R,

where  

R = Σ_{n=101}^{∞} d(n)·10^{100-n} = Σ_{k=1}^{∞} d(100+k)·10^{-k}.

Because d(100+k) ≤ 12 for k ≥ 1 (in fact ≤ 32 for all k, but the first few values are much smaller),
R < 0.3; hence 0 ≤ R < 1 and it contributes no integer part.  
Therefore

⌊10^{100}S⌋ = Σ_{n=1}^{100} d(n)·10^{100-n}.

The remainder modulo 1000 depends only on the terms whose powers are 10^{0}, 10^{1}, 10^{2}, i.e. n = 100, 99, 98:

• d(100) : 100 = 2^2·5^2  ⇒ d(100) = (2+1)(2+1) = 9  
• d(99)  : 99  = 3^2·11   ⇒ d(99)  = (2+1)(1+1) = 6  
• d(98)  : 98  = 2·7^2    ⇒ d(98)  = (1+1)(2+1) = 6  

Thus

remainder = 100·d(98) + 10·d(99) + d(100)
           = 100·6 + 10·6 + 9
           = 600 + 60 + 9
           = 669.

Hence the required remainder is

\boxed{669}.

Write the series in a form that reveals its decimal digits.

1/(10^k−1)=1/10^k·1/(1−10^(−k))  
     =∑_{m≥1}10^{−km}

Hence  

S=∑_{k≥1}1/(10^k−1)=∑_{k≥1}∑_{m≥1}10^{−km}
  =∑_{n≥1}d(n)·10^{−n},

where d(n) is the number of positive divisors of n.  
Thus the decimal expansion of S is

0.d(1)d(2)d(3)d(4)··· ,

except that carries have to be performed, because some d(n) exceed 9.

To obtain the remainder of ⌊10^{100}S⌋ modulo 1000 we need the 98-th, 99-th and 100-th digits after the decimal point once all carries from the right have been taken into account.

Carries can only come from digits far to the right.  
For n≥201

d(n)≤n ⇒ ∑_{n≥201}d(n)10^{−n}<∑_{n≥201}n10^{−n}
 <(10/9)·201·10^{−201}<2.3·10^{−198}.

Multiplying by 10^{200} gives <0.23, so no carry can reach the 200-th
digit.  Therefore it suffices to work with d(1)…d(200).

Start from n=200 and move leftwards, propagating the carry:

(Only the carry and the last three required digits are shown.)

 – n=200: 12 → digit 2, carry 1  
 – n=199: 2+1=3 → digit 3, carry 0  
 ⋯ (continue)  
 – n=101: 2 → digit 2, carry 0  

At this point the carry into the 100-th digit is 0.

 n=100: 9 → digit 9, carry 0  
 n=99 : 6 → digit 6, carry 0  
 n=98 : 6 → digit 6, carry 0  

Subsequent carries (from n≤97) move further left and cannot alter
digits 98–100.

Therefore

s_98 = 6, s_99 = 6, s_100 = 9,

and

⌊10^{100}S⌋ ≡ 100·s_98 + 10·s_99 + s_100
            ≡ 100·6 + 10·6 + 9
            ≡ 669  (mod 1000).

\boxed{669}

Step 1.  Express S as a double series  
For |10^{-n}|<1 we have  
1/(10^{n}-1)=Σ_{k=1}^{∞}10^{-nk}.  
Hence  

S = Σ_{n=1}^{∞}1/(10^{n}-1)  
   = Σ_{n=1}^{∞} Σ_{k=1}^{∞} 10^{-nk}  
   = Σ_{m=1}^{∞} d(m) ·10^{-m},  

where d(m) is the number of positive divisors of m (because each pair (n,k) with nk=m contributes 1).

So the decimal expansion of S is

0.d(1)d(2)d(3)d(4)… ,

before carrying is performed.

Step 2.  Shift the decimal point 100 places  
Multiplying by 10^{100} gives  

10^{100}S = Σ_{m=1}^{∞} d(m)·10^{100-m}  
          = (Σ_{m=1}^{100} d(m)·10^{100-m}) +  Σ_{m=101}^{∞} d(m)·10^{100-m}  
          = N₀ + F,

where  
N₀ = Σ_{m=1}^{100} d(m)·10^{100-m} (an integer composed of the first 100
decimal digits)  
F  = Σ_{t=1}^{∞} d(100+t)·10^{-t}  (<1, as shown below).

Step 3.  Show that F < 1 (no carry into N₀)  
For  t ≥1 the size of d(100+t) is never very large (in fact d(n)≤192 for n≤2000 and grows very slowly).  
A crude bound is d(n)≤64 for 101≤n≤200, so  

F < Σ_{t=1}^{∞} 64·10^{-t} = 64/9 < 8.  
A sharper computation with the actual values d(101)…d(120) gives  

F ≈ 0.28288432285… < 1.

Hence no carry propagates into N₀, and  

⌊10^{100}S⌋ = N₀.

Step 4.  The last three digits of N₀  
Because N₀’s decimal digits are precisely d(1), d(2), … , d(100), its
98-th, 99-th and 100-th digits are d(98), d(99) and d(100).

Compute these divisor counts.

• 98 = 2·7²  ⇒ d(98) = (1+1)(2+1) = 2·3 = 6  
• 99 = 3²·11  ⇒ d(99) = (2+1)(1+1) = 3·2 = 6  
• 100 = 2²·5² ⇒ d(100) = (2+1)(2+1) = 3·3 = 9

Thus the last three digits of ⌊10^{100}S⌋ are 6, 6, 9 – i.e. the number 669.

Step 5.  Required remainder  
Therefore the remainder when ⌊10^{100}S⌋ is divided by 1000 is

\boxed{669}.

Step 1:  Write the series in a more convenient form  
For n ≥ 1  
 1/(10ⁿ – 1)=10^{–n}/(1–10^{–n})=∑_{k=1}^{∞}10^{-nk}.  
Hence  

 S = Σ_{n=1}^{∞} 1/(10ⁿ – 1)  
  = Σ_{n=1}^{∞} Σ_{k=1}^{∞} 10^{-nk}.  

Step 2:  Exchange the order of summation  
For every positive integer m there are exactly d(m) ordered pairs (n,k) with nk = m, where d(m) is the divisor–counting function.  
Thus  

 S = Σ_{m=1}^{∞} d(m)·10^{-m}.                                        (1)

Step 3:  Multiply by 10^{100}  
10^{100}S = Σ_{m=1}^{∞} d(m)·10^{100-m}.                             (2)

For m ≤ 100 the terms in (2) are integers; for m > 100 they are strictly between 0 and 1.

Step 4:  Which of those integers affect the last three digits?  
If 100–m ≥ 3, the factor 10^{100-m} is a multiple of 1000, so it contributes 0 (mod 1000).  
Hence only m = 98, 99, 100 matter.

Compute the relevant divisor counts:  
 d(98)=6 (98=2·7²)  
 d(99)=6 (99=3²·11)  
 d(100)=9 (100=2²·5²)

Their contributions to 10^{100}S are  

 m=100 :  9·10^{0}=          9  
 m=99  :  6·10^{1}=         60  
 m=98  :  6·10^{2}=        600  

Sum (mod 1000): 669.                                             (3)

Step 5:  Estimate the fractional tail  
Let  

 F = Σ_{m=101}^{∞} d(m)·10^{100-m} = Σ_{t=1}^{∞} d(100+t)·10^{-t}.   (4)

A general bound d(n) ≤ √n gives, for t ≥ 2,  
 d(100+t) ≤ √(100+t) < √(10^{t}) = 10^{t/2}.  
Hence the t-th term is < 10^{t/2}·10^{-t}=10^{-t/2}.

Therefore  
 F < 2·10^{-1}+Σ_{t=2}^{∞}10^{-t/2}  
  =0.2+10^{-1}(1)/(1-10^{-1/2})  
  ≈0.2+0.262 = 0.462 < 1.                                        (5)

So 0 ≤ F < 1 and F contributes no additional integer to 10^{100}S.

Step 6:  Assemble the integer part  
⌊10^{100}S⌋ ≡ 669 (mod 1000).

Final answer  
\boxed{669}

Step 1.  Re–express the series  

  S = Σ_{n=1}^{∞} 1/(10^{n}-1).

For each n,  
1/(10^{n}-1) = 10^{-n}/(1-10^{-n}) = Σ_{k=1}^{∞} 10^{-nk}.  

So

  S = Σ_{n=1}^{∞} Σ_{k=1}^{∞} 10^{-nk}.

Put m = nk.  The pair (n,k) is just a factorisation of m, so it occurs τ(m) times, where τ(m) is the divisor–counting function.  Hence  

  S = Σ_{m=1}^{∞} τ(m)/10^{m}.  (★)

Step 2.  Multiply by 10^{100}:

  10^{100}S = Σ_{m=1}^{∞} τ(m)·10^{100-m}
              = Σ_{m=1}^{100} τ(m)·10^{100-m}  +  Σ_{m=101}^{∞} τ(m)·10^{100-m}  
              = I + F,

where I is the integer part and F the fractional part.

Step 3.  Show the fractional part is < 1.  
For m≥101 the power of 10 is at most 10^{-1}.  Since τ(m) ≤ 12 for 100≤m≤120 and grows very slowly, a crude bound gives

   F < Σ_{e=1}^{∞} (20)/10^{e} = 20·(1/10)/(1-1/10) = 20/9 < 1/2.

So F cannot create an extra integer; floor(10^{100}S) = I.

Step 4.  To get I (mod 1000) we only need the terms whose 10–power is <10^{3}, i.e. those with 100-m <3 ⇒ m = 98, 99, 100.

10^{100-98}=10^{2}, 10^{100-99}=10^{1}, 10^{100-100}=10^{0}.

Compute τ(m):

• 98 = 2·7^{2}  τ(98)=(1+1)(2+1)=6  
• 99 = 3^{2}·11  τ(99)=(2+1)(1+1)=6  
• 100 = 2^{2}·5^{2} τ(100)=(2+1)(2+1)=9  

Thus  

I ≡ 6·10^{2} + 6·10^{1} + 9·10^{0}  (mod 1000)  
  ≡ 600 + 60 + 9  
  ≡ 669  (mod 1000).

Step 5.  Since F < 1, floor(10^{100}S) has the same last three digits as I.

Hence the required remainder is  

\boxed{669}.

Step 1.  Turn each summand into a geometric series  
For n ≥ 1,
1/(10ⁿ − 1)  
= 1/[10ⁿ(1 − 10⁻ⁿ)]  
= 10⁻ⁿ · ∑_{k=0}^{∞}10^{-nk}  
= ∑_{k=1}^{∞}10^{-nk}.

Step 2.  Write S as a single series  
S = ∑_{n=1}^{∞}∑_{k=1}^{∞}10^{-nk}.  
Let m = nk.  For a fixed m, the number of ordered pairs (n,k) with nk = m is the number of positive divisors of m, denoted d(m).  Hence

S = ∑_{m=1}^{∞} d(m)·10^{-m}.             (1)

Step 3.  Isolate the integer part of 10¹⁰⁰S  
Multiply (1) by 10¹⁰⁰:

10¹⁰⁰S = ∑_{m=1}^{∞} d(m)·10^{100-m}.    (2)

The summands with 100 − m ≥ 3 contain a factor 10³ and therefore contribute 0 modulo 1000.  Only the terms with exponents 2, 1, 0 matter, i.e. m = 98, 99, 100.

Step 4.  Evaluate the needed divisor counts  
98 = 2·7² ⇒ d(98) = (1+1)(2+1) = 6  
99 = 3²·11 ⇒ d(99) = (2+1)(1+1) = 6  
100 = 2²·5² ⇒ d(100) = (2+1)(2+1) = 9

Step 5.  Form the residue modulo 1000  
Contribution from m = 98: 6·10² = 600  
Contribution from m = 99: 6·10¹ =  60  
Contribution from m = 100: 9·10⁰ =   9  
Sum: 600 + 60 + 9 = 669.

Step 6.  Verify no carry from the fractional part  
For m ≥ 101, the term d(m)·10^{100-m} ≤ d(m)·10⁻¹ ≤ (101)·10⁻¹ < 1/2 for the first term and decays exponentially thereafter, so the entire tail of (2) is < 1.  Hence it cannot increase the integer part, and the calculation in Step 5 is unaffected.

Therefore
⌊10¹⁰⁰S⌋ ≡ 669 (mod 1000).

\boxed{669}

t   d(100+t)    contribution
1      2         0.2
2      8         0.08
3      2         0.002
4      8         0.0008
5      8         0.00008
6      4         0.000004

floor(10^{100}S)=A+floor(B)=A,

1/(10ⁿ−1)=0.\overline{00…01}  (a ‘1’ appears every n places).

k : (dₖ before carry) → (digit after carry)

200 : 12 → 2  (carry 1)  
199 : 2+1=3 → 3  (carry 0)  
198 : 12 → 2  (carry 1)  
197 : 2+1=3 → 3  (carry 0)  
⋯

100 : 9 → 9  (carry 0)  
 99 : 6 → 6  (carry 0)  
 98 : 6 → 6  (carry 0)  
 97 : 2 → 2  (carry 0)  
⋯  

(Beyond k = 200 every dₖ ≤ 8, so no further carries can reach k = 100.)

d₉₈ = 6 ,  d₉₉ = 6 ,  d₁₀₀ = 9.

\boxed{669}.

Because the function π must be onto A={1,…,6}, it is necessarily a permutation of the six elements.  
Write its cycle decomposition.  For a permutation, π⁶ = id  iff  the least common multiple of its cycle‐lengths divides 6.  
Hence every cycle can have length only 1, 2, 3 or 6 and the multiset of cycle lengths must sum to 6.

All possible cycle–type partitions of 6 are  

1) 1⁶      (identity)  
2) 2·1⁴      
3) 2²·1²     
4) 2³       
5) 3·1³      
6) 3·2·1    (lcm = 6)  
7) 3²       
8) 6      

For a type 1^{a₁} 2^{a₂} 3^{a₃} 6^{a₆}, the number of permutations is  

6! / (1^{a₁} a₁! · 2^{a₂} a₂! · 3^{a₃} a₃! · 6^{a₆} a₆!).

Compute each:

1) 1⁶:  720 / (1⁶·6!) = 1  
2) 2·1⁴: 720 / (1⁴·4!·2¹·1!) = 15  
3) 2²·1²: 720 / (1²·2!·2²·2!) = 45  
4) 2³:  720 / (2³·3!) = 15  
5) 3·1³: 720 / (1³·3!·3¹·1!) = 40  
6) 3·2·1: 720 / (1¹·1!·2¹·1!·3¹·1!) = 120  
7) 3²:  720 / (3²·2!) = 40  
8) 6:   720 / (6¹·1!) = 120  

Sum: 1 + 15 + 45 + 15 + 40 + 120 + 40 + 120 = 396.

Hence the number of onto functions π with π⁶ = id is 

\boxed{396}.

Step 1.  A surjective function from a finite set to itself is necessarily bijective, so the required functions are exactly the permutations π of the set  
A = {1,2,3,4,5,6}.

Step 2.  The condition π⁶ = id means the order of π divides 6.  
Hence every cycle of π must have length 1, 2, 3 or 6 and the least common multiple (l.c.m.) of those lengths must divide 6.

Step 3.  List all possible cycle–type partitions of 6 using only the allowed lengths:

1) 1⁶  
2) 2 1⁴  
3) 2 2 1²  
4) 2 2 2  
5) 3 1³  
6) 3 2 1  
7) 3 3  
8) 6

Step 4.  For each type compute the number of permutations.  
For a cycle type with m₁ 1-cycles, m₂ 2-cycles, … the number is

6! / (m₁! m₂! … 1^{m₁} 2^{m₂} …).

Cycle type  (m₁,m₂,m₃,m₆)  Count  
1) 1⁶  (6,0,0,0)  6!/6! = 1  
2) 2 1⁴  (4,1,0,0)  6!/(4!·2) = 15  
3) 2 2 1² (2,2,0,0)  6!/(2!·2!·2²) = 45  
4) 2 2 2  (0,3,0,0)  6!/(3!·2³) = 15  
5) 3 1³  (3,0,1,0)  6!/(3!·3) = 40  
6) 3 2 1  (1,1,1,0)  6!/(1!·1!·1!·2·3) = 120  
7) 3 3  (0,0,2,0)  6!/(2!·3²) = 40  
8) 6  (0,0,0,1)  6!/6 = 120

Step 5.  Add the totals:

1 + 15 + 45 + 15 + 40 + 120 + 40 + 120 = 396.

Therefore, the number of onto functions π with π⁶ = id is  

\boxed{396}.

For a map \(\pi : A \to A\) on the finite set \(A=\{1,2,3,4,5,6\}\)

1.  Onto  \(\Longrightarrow\) bijective, so \(\pi\) is a permutation of the 6 elements.

2.  The condition \(\pi^{6}= \text{id}\) means that, in the cycle decomposition of the permutation, every element returns to itself after 6 iterations.  
   Therefore every cycle length \(m\) must divide \(6\).  
   Allowed cycle lengths: \(1,2,3,6\).

3.  List all partitions of 6 using only these lengths and count the permutations of each type.

   a) \(1+1+1+1+1+1\): \(k_1=6\)   
      Number \(= \dfrac{6!}{1^6\,6!}=1\)

   b) \(2+1+1+1+1\): \(k_2=1,\;k_1=4\)  
      Number \(= \dfrac{6!}{1^4\,4!\;2^1\,1!}=15\)

   c) \(2+2+1+1\): \(k_2=2,\;k_1=2\)  
      Number \(= \dfrac{6!}{1^2\,2!\;2^2\,2!}=45\)

   d) \(2+2+2\): \(k_2=3\)  
      Number \(= \dfrac{6!}{2^3\,3!}=15\)

   e) \(3+1+1+1\): \(k_3=1,\;k_1=3\)  
      Number \(= \dfrac{6!}{1^3\,3!\;3^1\,1!}=40\)

   f) \(3+2+1\): \(k_3=1,\;k_2=1,\;k_1=1\)  
      Number \(= \dfrac{6!}{1^1\,1!\;2^1\,1!\;3^1\,1!}=120\)

   g) \(3+3\): \(k_3=2\)  
      Number \(= \dfrac{6!}{3^2\,2!}=40\)

   h) \(6\): \(k_6=1\)  
      Number \(= \dfrac{6!}{6^1\,1!}=120\)

4.  Sum all possibilities:
   \(1+15+45+15+40+120+40+120 = 396\).

Hence the number of onto functions \(\pi\) with \(\pi^{6}=\text{id}\) is

\boxed{396}

Step 1.  Reformulate the problem  
Because the map π is from the six–element set A onto itself, “onto’’ forces π to be a bijection; hence π is a permutation of the set {1,2,3,4,5,6}.  
The condition  
  π∘π∘π∘π∘π∘π = id  
is the same as π⁶ = id, i.e. the order of π divides 6.

Step 2.  Possible cycle structures  
For a permutation of six elements, the multiset of cycle-lengths must sum to 6, and their least common multiple must divide 6.  
Thus every cycle length must be 1, 2, 3 or 6, and the admissible partitions of 6 are

1) 1+1+1+1+1+1  (1⁶)  
2) 2+1+1+1+1   (2·1⁴)  
3) 2+2+1+1    (2²·1²)  
4) 2+2+2     (2³)  
5) 3+1+1+1    (3·1³)  
6) 3+3      (3²)
